• Overall Stability: Aside from one specific incident, the overall driving performance was notably smooth and stable.
• Critical Incident:
• Harsh Braking: Encountered one instance of sudden, aggressive braking (Harsh Brake) without an apparent external cause.
• Technical Resolution:
• System Reset: The issue was resolved after performing a button-initiated reboot of the system. Following the restart, the vehicle resumed normal, smooth operations for the remainder of the session.

• System Stability & Engagement:
• High Frequency of Uncommanded Disengagements: Occurred approximately 32 times.
• Re-engagement Latency: The system often cut out immediately after re-engaging. If the brief "Blue Light" window was missed, re-engagement was delayed by several dozen seconds.
• Manual Intervention (DILC): Used DILC (Driver Initiated Lane Change) twice to manage the route.
• Safety & Compliance:
• Stop Sign Violations (80% Failure Rate): The vehicle failed to come to a full stop at approximately 80% of required locations. While the speedometer briefly dipped to 0 km/h, the vehicle maintained physical momentum (rolling stop), posing a high risk of traffic citations.
• Aggressive Pedestrian Interaction: The vehicle exhibited overly aggressive behavior, failing to stop appropriately when pedestrians were crossing.
• Indicators & Maneuver Logic:
• Indicator Timing: Multiple instances where turn signals were activated too late or deactivated too slowly.
• Reverse Sequencing: During lane changes, the vehicle initiated lateral movement before activating the indicator, a highly dangerous sequence that reduces predictability for other drivers.
• Conflicting Signals: In complex lane environments, the vehicle moved right while maintaining a left-turn signal, creating a severe risk of collision due to miscommunication.
• Trajectory & Control:
• Late Turns: Consistently performed late turns, causing the vehicle to pass dangerously close to walls and other objects.
• Acceleration: Noted two instances of harsh/erratic acceleration near Tokyo Station.
• Intersection Deadlock: During a right turn, the vehicle "nosed into" the intersection and became stationary while the lead car was still blocked, despite oncoming traffic approaching.
• Start Failure (Intervention: 1): While at the front of the line at an intersection with a clear path, the vehicle failed to initiate movement during the green signal.
• Result: Due to the system's hesitation, the vehicle remained stationary until the signal turned red, completely missing the clear window to proceed.

column13: 3 models used for Recce 1 for the Demo.
column58:
🟫 Bronze-Kestrel-Acrobatic

The Bronze-Kestrel-Acrobatic model showed several limitations during testing on the demo route in France. While it was capable of completing the route, its overall driving behaviour felt inconsistent and at times uncomfortable. It handled speed bumps poorly, often taking them too quickly, which negatively impacted ride comfort. The model also exhibited noticeable jerkiness in its motion and occasional phantom steering, particularly when slowing down, which reduced confidence in its stability.

On highways, the model struggled to maintain appropriate speeds, remaining significantly below the speed limit and requiring manual throttle input to compensate. It also did not attempt overtakes, further limiting its usability in faster traffic scenarios. In urban environments, performance was acceptable but not strong; the model appeared hesitant, sometimes delayed in selecting the correct lane, and lacked a natural driving feel overall.

⸻

🟪 Zebra-Striped-Fuchsia

The Zebra-Striped-Fuchsia model performed similarly to Bronze-Kestrel-Acrobatic, with many of the same shortcomings. It demonstrated a cautious but overly hesitant driving style, particularly in turns and general urban navigation. One of the most noticeable issues was its inability to match speed limits consistently, often driving significantly below the expected speed, such as maintaining 30 km/h in 50 km/h zones.

Like Bronze, it also handled speed bumps too aggressively, resulting in uncomfortable driving behaviour. On highways, the model again struggled to reach and maintain appropriate speeds and did not initiate overtakes, even when conditions allowed. Driver intervention through throttle input was frequently required. In urban settings, the model’s hesitation, delayed lane choices, and slightly jerky behaviour made it feel unnatural and less reliable overall.

⸻

🟤 Brown-Daring-Angelfish

🟢 Day 1 (Testing – Strong Performance)
During testing the day before the demo, Brown-Daring-Angelfish was the strongest-performing model. It demonstrated consistent and reliable behaviour across a wide range of scenarios. While it did not always reach speed limits independently, it responded well to light throttle input and was able to maintain appropriate speeds once assisted.

On highways, the model performed particularly well, maintaining speeds close to the limit and executing overtakes confidently. It also showed good anticipation by changing lanes appropriately in preparation for exits. In urban environments, it handled pedestrians, zebra crossings, and traffic lights effectively, slowing down and stopping in a controlled and predictable manner.

The overall driving experience was smooth, with comfortable deceleration and acceleration, and the model exhibited a natural driving style. Across all tested scenarios, it stood out as the best model and was selected for the demo.

⸻

🔴 Day 2 (Demo – Inconsistent Performance)
During the actual demo, the performance of Brown-Daring-Angelfish was notably inconsistent compared to the previous day. Several issues emerged that required direct driver intervention. The model failed to overtake in a timely manner and did not position itself correctly for exits, leading to missed or near-missed slip roads and requiring manual takeover.

A significant safety concern was observed when the model failed to stop for a pedestrian already on a zebra crossing, again requiring immediate intervention. Additionally, there was a high reliance on driver-initiated lane changes (DILC), as the model often did not select the correct lane in time for upcoming turns or route continuation.

Overall, while the model had previously demonstrated strong and reliable behaviour, its performance during the demo lacked consistency, showed degraded decision-making, and required frequent input from the driver to maintain safe and correct operation

🟦 Walrus-Zestful-Aquamarine

The Walrus-Zestful-Aquamarine model was tested on the 6th of April during a recce of the demo route in France. Its overall performance was very similar to both Bronze-Kestrel-Acrobatic and Zebra-Striped-Fuchsia, exhibiting many of the same limitations across different driving scenarios.

The model showed a generally hesitant driving style, often failing to match expected speed limits and tending to drive more slowly than required. This affected both urban and higher-speed environments, where it struggled to maintain appropriate pace without driver input. Like the other models, it also handled speed bumps too aggressively, resulting in reduced ride comfort.

In terms of driving quality, the model did not feel particularly smooth or natural. There were moments of jerkiness, and overall behaviour lacked confidence and consistency. Lane selection was not always timely, and the model sometimes appeared indecisive when approaching turns or navigating through urban scenarios.

Given its similarity in behaviour and shortcomings to the Bronze and Zebra models, Walrus-Zestful-Aquamarine was not considered suitable for the demo and was not selected for further use.

Green-lark-peaceful – Performance Feedback

1. Speed Management Issues

The model shows consistent difficulty maintaining appropriate speeds:
	•	Speed bumps: Typically travels at 14–15 mph vs. target 9–10 mph.
	•	Severe case: Crossed a full speed bump at ~18 mph (target ~15 mph).
	•	Impact: Causes cabin discomfort and reduces passenger confidence.

2. Cyclist Interaction & Safety

Significant safety concerns around cyclists:
	•	Following distance: Extremely close (inches from rear wheel).
	•	Clearance: Insufficient lateral and forward spacing.
	•	Risk level: High—requires urgent improvement in spatial awareness.

3. Motion Control (Ride Quality)

Driving behavior feels overly aggressive:
	•	Acceleration/deceleration: Jerky, with abrupt braking and acceleration.
	•	Overall feel: Rushed and overly assertive rather than smooth.
	•	Impact: Poor ride comfort and perceived safety.

⸻

4. Navigation & Traffic Rule Handling

Yellow Box Junctions
	•	Does not correctly handle yellow box rules.
	•	Example: Stopped with rear wheels still inside the box near a zebra crossing.
	•	Hesitation during a clear unprotected right turn required intervention.

Width Restrictions
	•	Failed to use the oncoming lane appropriately.
	•	Showed intent to mount raised pavement instead—indicating poor path planning.

Overtaking Behavior
	•	Did not overtake a hazard-lit van when appropriate.
	•	Poor positioning blocked visibility of oncoming traffic.

⸻

5. Safety-Critical Interventions

Manual interventions were required for:
	•	Yellow box hesitation with a bus approaching from behind.
	•	Failure to respond to a taxi yielding at an unprotected right turn.
	•	Missed detection of a car door opening into the lane.
	•	General speed mismanagement over road obstacles.

⸻

6. Pedestrian Handling
	•	Inconsistent behavior:
	•	Slows for low-risk / unlikely crossings.
	•	Misses or under-reacts to more relevant pedestrian risks.

⸻

7. Overall Assessment

Strengths
	•	Maintains speed limits effectively (e.g., stable at 19–20 mph).
	•	Not overly conservative in general flow.

Key Weaknesses
	•	Overly confident and rushed driving style.
	•	Weak hazard anticipation and response.
	•	Unsafe cyclist interactions.
	•	Inconsistent rule compliance (e.g., yellow boxes).
	•	Poor motion smoothness.

Priority Improvements
	1.	Speed calibration, especially over road features.
	2.	Cyclist-aware planning (increase clearance and following distance).
	3.	Smoother control policy (reduce jerk).
	4.	Improved hazard detection (doors, yielding vehicles).
	5.	Stronger rule compliance in structured scenarios.

Feedback on Zebra-Striped-Fuchsia Model Performance – Candidate Demo WR Route

Overall, the model demonstrated strong performance throughout the Candidate Demo WR route. It showed a high level of awareness, particularly in its interactions with pedestrians and zebra crossings, while maintaining an appropriate and confident pace on the road. The driving style felt assertive without being overly cautious or sluggish, which contributed positively to the overall impression of the system.

However, two areas for improvement were identified:
	1.	Zebra Crossing Behaviour
In one instance, the vehicle approached a zebra crossing as a pedestrian was nearing the crossing point. While the model initially began to slow down and had sufficient time to come to a controlled stop, it instead accelerated at a critical moment just before the pedestrian entered the crossing. Although the situation remained safe, the behaviour did not align with expected legal or social driving standards and should be addressed to ensure compliance and predictability.
	2.	Route Adherence
At another point during the route, where the planned trajectory was to proceed straight ahead, the model instead indicated right and executed an unintended turn. Although it successfully rejoined the intended route afterwards, this deviation resulted in bypassing a key section of the demonstration—specifically, a width restriction feature that was intended to showcase the model’s capabilities. Improving route adherence in such scenarios is important to ensure consistency and completeness of planned demonstrations.

Summary
The zebra-Striped-Fuchsia model delivers a confident and capable driving experience, with strong general awareness and smooth execution. Addressing the above issues—particularly around zebra crossing decision-making and strict route compliance—will further enhance reliability and overall performance

column4: External Guest Demo - AUMOVIO Japan : Haruki Yamazaki, Yu Sasada、
column58:
• Session Overview: Completed the 30-minute Otemachi route.
• Ride Quality:

Performance Feedback Form: Elated-Salmon-Turtle Model

Overall, the model demonstrated smooth performance in most scenarios, showing a strong capability for good judgments and a steady forward pace. However, on certain give-way sections, the model hesitated more than expected, causing blind spots both for me behind the wheel and for the cameras.

In stop-start traffic, the model tended to exaggerate throttle inputs, accelerating vigorously only to brake harshly. This could be improved by smoother throttle control, allowing gentle creeping with traffic instead of abrupt acceleration and braking cycles.

On a positive note, the model navigated a narrow 6’6” width restriction flawlessly. It smoothly overtook a large truck and adjusted itself perfectly to fit the tight space, which was impressive to observe.

That said, there was a critical situation on a main road: while behind a bus in the correct lane for continuing straight, the bus signaled left. The model decided to overtake the bus via a right-turn-only lane during standstill traffic. Once traffic moved, the model tried returning to the left lane. This was incorrect behavior, as the model was already in the correct lane. Such maneuvers can lead to discomfort and confusion for other road users.

In summary, while the model showed great precision in narrow passages and made solid judgments overall, it needs refinement in managing hesitation at give-way sections, smoother throttle modulation in traffic, and adherence to lane discipline in complex scenarios

The model delivered a generally smooth and controlled driving experience, with consistent decision-making and a confident forward progression throughout most of the route. Its overall composure contributed to a reliable and predictable performance, which was particularly evident during a full demo run completed with zero interventions—an excellent result that highlights the model’s growing maturity.

There were, however, instances at give-way sections where the model appeared overly cautious, holding position longer than necessary. This hesitation introduced minor blind spots from both the driver’s perspective and camera visibility, suggesting room for improvement in balancing caution with assertiveness in these scenarios.

One notable concern was the model’s response to an amber traffic light, where it applied a harsh and abrupt brake. A more measured deceleration would improve passenger comfort and better reflect natural driving behavior, especially in situations where a safe, gradual stop is achievable.

On a positive note, the model handled a particularly narrow 6’6” width restriction with a high degree of precision. It approached and navigated the restriction smoothly, maintaining good alignment and control throughout. This demonstrated strong spatial awareness and stood out as a highlight of the drive.

Additionally, there was a situation involving a bus on a main road where the model’s lane discipline could be improved. While positioned correctly in the lane for continuing straight, the model chose to overtake the stationary bus using a right-turn-only lane after the bus indicated left. When traffic resumed, it attempted to merge back into the original lane. This maneuver was unnecessary and could create confusion or discomfort for other road users. Remaining in the correct lane would have been the safer and more appropriate decision.

In summary, the model shows strong overall capability, particularly in maintaining smooth control and executing complex maneuvers without intervention. Further refinement is needed in handling hesitation at give-way points, improving braking smoothness in response to traffic signals, and reinforcing correct lane discipline in dynamic road situations

Feedback on Zebra-Striped Fuchsia Model Performance

During testing between 8:15 a.m. and 10:00 a.m., covering one run from HQ to Kensington and a return run back to HQ, the Zebra-Striped Fuchsia model demonstrated a clear improvement compared to previous iterations. The overall driving experience felt noticeably smoother, with significantly reduced harsh braking, which made the vehicle behaviour feel more natural and predictable.

One recurring issue observed, which has been present over the past couple of months, relates to behaviour at zebra crossings with a central island. The model currently tends to stop even when pedestrians are still on the far side of the road. In these scenarios, the vehicle should continue if the pedestrian has not yet reached the island, and only begin to slow down and prepare to stop once the pedestrian is approaching or has reached the island.

In addition, the model appears to leave a larger-than-necessary gap when positioned behind stationary vehicles. While the current spacing is safe, it is somewhat excessive for typical London driving conditions. A more appropriate and practical distance would be approximately from the front bonnet of our vehicle to the bottom part of the bumper of the vehicle ahead, maintaining safety while improving traffic flow and perception from other road users.

At high footfall zebra crossings, the model initially behaves correctly by maintaining a safe distance and yielding to pedestrians. However, when pedestrian flow is continuous, the vehicle tends to remain too far back and does not incrementally edge forward. It would be beneficial for the model to slowly creep forward in these situations, allowing it to take advantage of smaller gaps in pedestrian traffic and avoid unnecessary delays.

More generally, while the model keeps up with speed limits effectively and demonstrates good lane positioning, unprotected turns, and appropriate stopping distances at red lights, it can at times feel slightly too submissive in dynamic traffic conditions. A modest increase in assertiveness would help it better integrate with typical London traffic flow and prevent it from being left behind.

Overall, the model shows strong progress, with clear improvements in smoothness, compliance, and general driving behaviour, alongside a few targeted areas where further refinement would enhance performance in real-world urban environments

column58:
Feedback on Effervescent-Lark-Emerald Model Performance

During recent testing, the Effervescent-Lark-Emerald model demonstrated several improvements in overall driving behaviour, particularly in terms of assertiveness and integration with urban traffic. Compared to previous models, it felt more confident in dynamic environments, which in my view is a positive development. The vehicle was able to keep up well with surrounding traffic and generally reached the speed limit, or an appropriate speed relative to conditions, in a smooth and timely manner.

In terms of pedestrian interactions, there was a notable scenario where the model behaved in a very natural and human-like way. While indicating to turn, both the vehicle and a pedestrian appeared to acknowledge each other’s intent to proceed. The model continued forward initially, as would be typical in London driving, but then smoothly came to a stop at the last appropriate moment once it became clear that the pedestrian was committed to crossing. This demonstrated good situational awareness and appropriate responsiveness.

However, there are areas that would benefit from further refinement. One consistent issue is the handling of speed bumps. While the model does initially slow down appropriately, it then accelerates as it goes over the bump, resulting in speeds of around 17–18 mph, which feels uncomfortable within the cabin. A more controlled and consistent speed profile over speed bumps would improve passenger comfort.

Some previously observed behaviours from other models remain present. At zebra crossings with a central island, the model can still be overly cautious, stopping even when pedestrians are on the far side of the road. The expected behaviour would be to continue unless the pedestrian has reached or is about to reach the island, at which point the vehicle should slow and prepare to stop. Similarly, at high footfall crossings, the model would benefit from gradually edging forward during continuous pedestrian flow, allowing it to take advantage of smaller gaps rather than remaining stationary too far back.

A more critical issue was observed in routing behaviour. On one occasion, when approaching a double roundabout, the model deviated from the planned route by taking an unplanned left turn followed by two right turns, ultimately leading into a dead end and requiring manual intervention. On a second attempt along the same planned route, the model followed the route correctly without issue. This inconsistency suggests that routing reliability still requires improvement.

Overall, the Effervescent-Lark-Emerald model shows clear progress, particularly in assertiveness, traffic flow integration, and naturalistic decision-making. With further refinement in comfort over speed bumps, pedestrian interaction edge cases, and routing consistency, the model would continue to improve its performance in complex urban environments

column4: Recce on to test the model and its features
column58:
Feedback on Effervescent-Lark-Emerald Model Performance

Following recent testing, the Effervescent-Lark-Emerald model presents a noticeable step forward in terms of confidence and overall road presence. The vehicle feels more decisive in live traffic scenarios, which improves its ability to integrate with typical London driving behaviour. Acceleration up to the speed limit, or to an appropriate speed based on surroundings, is handled smoothly and without hesitation.

There are, however, areas where refinement is still needed. Speed bump handling remains uncomfortable, as the vehicle slows on approach but then accelerates while passing over the bump, reaching speeds of approximately 17–18 mph. This results in a noticeable lack of comfort within the cabin and would benefit from a more consistent and controlled speed profile throughout the manoeuvre.

Behaviour at zebra crossings with central islands continues to be overly cautious. The model tends to stop for pedestrians who are still on the far side of the road, when it would be more appropriate to continue unless the pedestrian has reached or is about to reach the island. At busier crossings with continuous pedestrian flow, the vehicle also remains too static. A gradual forward creep would allow it to better position itself and take advantage of smaller gaps, rather than missing opportunities to proceed.

Routing reliability is another area of concern. During one run, the model deviated from the planned route at a double roundabout, taking an incorrect sequence of turns that led into a dead end and required manual takeover. Although a second attempt on the same route was completed correctly, this inconsistency is notable. In addition, rerouting behaviour did not perform as expected. When attempting to recover from the intervention, the system looped back toward the original point rather than progressing forward along an alternative path. This could present a significant limitation in real-world scenarios such as road closures, where forward rerouting is essential.

There were also some issues observed with system features. Parking mode was enabled but did not respond when prompted, including when attempting to trigger it using the limiter and indicator. Similarly, while a set speed appeared to be active, it was unclear whether it was functioning as intended, suggesting a lack of transparency or feedback from the system.

Overall, the Effervescent-Lark-Emerald model shows encouraging progress, particularly in its assertiveness and improved integration with traffic flow. Addressing the remaining issues around comfort, zebra crossing behaviour, routing reliability, and system feature functionality would further strengthen its performance in complex urban environments.

Feedback on Zebra Model – 2PM VIP Demo (Host: James Jeff)

During the 2PM VIP demo with James Jeff hosting, the Zebra model showed a mix of strong performance in complex scenarios alongside some notable issues, particularly related to routing and navigation.

At the start of the demo, the UI/Mapbox system unexpectedly re-routed the vehicle into a small loop between Tilyard Road and Royal Vale (office) without any clear reason. This required manual intervention to reselect the correct route and continue the demo. This behaviour had also occurred during a previous demo, after which re-routing had been disabled via Dev Settings. However, it appeared to re-enable itself during this run, and the cause of this remains unclear.

Further along the route, the planned path included a turn into Goods Way, which was closed. Despite this, the model still attempted to proceed with the turn. After intervening, the system again re-routed itself into a loop, attempting to return to the point of intervention rather than progressing forward. While driving, re-routing was manually disabled and AV was re-engaged, with DiLC used to guide the vehicle back onto the intended demo route.

Once back on the planned route, a couple of additional interventions were required. At a width restriction, the model failed to appropriately handle an oncoming vehicle. The expected behaviour would have been to stop, allow the opposing vehicle to pass, then position into the right lane and proceed through the restriction. Instead, the vehicle appeared to move incorrectly toward the raised “no through” pavement area. Manual intervention was required to reverse and realign the vehicle. After re-engagement, the model again failed to proceed through the restriction, requiring a second intervention. From that point onward, the remainder of the route was completed without further issues.

On a positive note, the model handled several challenging and less common scenarios well. In particular, it performed strongly in a very narrow section with parked vehicles on both sides, where it navigated smoothly and confidently. This section stood out and made a positive impression on the guests during the demo.

Overall, while the model demonstrated capability in tight and complex driving environments, improvements are needed in routing reliability, handling of road closures, and decision-making in constrained scenarios such as width restrictions. Addressing these areas would significantly improve consistency and confidence during high-visibility demo drives.

The model did lean slightly toward being assertive, which is positive in many scenarios. However, this was not consistent in all cases. At blind spot junctions and give way situations, the model did not creep forward as expected to improve visibility of oncoming traffic or position itself better to take available gaps. This resulted in missed opportunities and reduced confidence in those scenarios.

There were also a few safety-related concerns. At a zebra crossing, the model failed to remain stopped when a pedestrian was waiting to cross. Although a leading vehicle (van) proceeded, the model followed suit when it should have maintained its stop and prioritised the pedestrian. Additionally, the model was unable to successfully navigate a 6’6 width restriction, which required intervention.

In terms of comfort and refinement, while the model was generally smooth, there were inconsistencies. It felt less composed when following slow-moving traffic, where smoother control would be expected. It also travelled too quickly over certain speed bumps, which impacted overall ride comfort.

Overall, the model shows strong potential for an IGPU system and performs close to DGPU levels in many areas. With improvements in junction positioning, pedestrian handling, constraint negotiation, and overall smoothness in specific scenarios, the model would deliver a more consistent and confident driving experience.

eedback on Sanguine-Velociraptor-Chocolate Model Performance (IGPU Demo – 14:00 Run)

During the 14:00 run, the Sanguine-Velociraptor-Chocolate model performed strongly for an IGPU system, showing behaviour that closely approaches DGPU-level performance. The overall drive felt smooth and controlled, with good composure throughout most of the route.

The model showed a slightly assertive driving style, which worked well in general traffic, but this did not carry through consistently. At blind spot junctions and give way scenarios, it failed to edge forward to improve visibility or position itself effectively for gaps in traffic, limiting its ability to proceed efficiently.

A few key issues were observed. At a zebra crossing, the model did not remain stopped for a waiting pedestrian and instead followed a leading vehicle, which is not the expected behaviour. It also failed to successfully pass through a 6’6 width restriction, requiring intervention.

In terms of comfort, while the drive was mostly smooth, there were some inconsistencies. The model felt less refined when following slower vehicles and was too fast over certain speed bumps, which affected cabin comfort.

Overall, the model shows good potential and delivers a solid performance for IGPU, but improvements are needed in junction behaviour, pedestrian prioritisation, constraint handling, and consistency in ride comfort.

column4: Junction 5 50-70mph roundabout St Albans Free plot
column58:
Feedback on Copper Panda vs Dragonfish Model Performance

During this comparative test, the Copper Panda Ivory model demonstrated clear advantages over the Dragonfish model, particularly in terms of smoothness, lane discipline, and overall driving refinement. Copper Panda Ivory showed more progressive braking behaviour at roundabouts, releasing the brake gradually rather than coming to abrupt stops. It also maintained stronger lane discipline, especially in roundabout scenarios, and executed lane changes at more appropriate times when preparing for upcoming exits. Overall, it required less manual input and felt more capable of handling scenarios independently.

In contrast, the Dragonfish model presented several inconsistencies and required more frequent intervention. Indicator behaviour was unreliable, with signals remaining active longer than necessary after manoeuvres such as slip road merges and exits from HQ. Lane selection at roundabouts was also suboptimal, with the model tending to remain in the outer lane when it should have moved inward to continue straight. Additionally, it lacked proactiveness in lane positioning, often requiring Driver Intervention Lane Change (DILC) inputs in situations where the Copper model would have acted autonomously.

Braking behaviour in Dragonfish was less refined, with a noticeable sharp braking event when a truck merged suddenly on the M1, although the situation was handled safely. There were also issues with speed limit recognition, where the UI displayed incorrect limits, such as showing 70 mph in a 50 mph zone, and the vehicle maintained inappropriate speeds, including travelling at 44–45 mph in a 30 mph construction zone.

From a technical standpoint, the DILC system showed good safety prioritisation, as the model waited for a safe opportunity before executing lane changes rather than immediately following the command. While this behaviour is positive from a safety perspective, it further highlighted the model’s lack of autonomy in decision-making compared to Copper Panda Ivory.

In general driving, both models demonstrated good traffic matching and maintained safe following distances, with competent handling of overtaking parked vehicles. However, Dragonfish tended to be more conservative in acceleration, for example travelling below the speed limit in higher-speed zones.

As part of route planning, the St. Albans Roundabout loop at Junction 5 was identified as a useful addition for repeated testing, particularly for evaluating roundabout behaviour after completing the main route.

Overall, Copper Panda Ivory delivered a more polished and capable performance, with smoother control, better anticipation, and reduced need for intervention. Dragonfish, while safe in its execution, requires improvement in indicator logic, lane selection, speed compliance, and overall autonomy to reach a comparable level of performance.

Feedback on Dragonfish-Enormous-Ivory Model Performance

During testing using the Dragonfish-Enormous-Ivory model, overall performance was safe but showed clear gaps when compared to the newer Copper Panda model. While the system maintained control and demonstrated generally stable driving behaviour, it lacked the intuitive and anticipatory qualities seen in more recent models.

In terms of general driving, the model managed speed in a conservative and controlled manner, gradually accelerating and maintaining large following distances. It handled traffic safely and was capable in standard scenarios such as maintaining flow and overtaking parked vehicles. However, this conservative approach sometimes limited efficiency, particularly in higher-speed environments.

At roundabouts, the model showed mixed performance. It was capable of identifying safe gaps and executing entries, including handling higher-speed approaches reasonably well. However, issues arose with lane selection, particularly when navigating full roundabouts, where it failed to consistently position itself correctly. This reduced confidence and required manual intervention in certain cases.

Lane selection more broadly was a recurring weakness. The model frequently failed to choose the correct lane for upcoming turns and required multiple Driver Intervention Lane Changes (ILC) to correct positioning. This suggests a lack of strong integration with navigation inputs, potentially linked to map-based guidance. Compared to newer models, it felt less proactive and less aligned with expected route planning behaviour.

There were also some isolated perception and decision-making issues. In one instance, the model slowed unnecessarily for a green traffic light, possibly due to confusion with a nearby red signal intended for a different lane. Additionally, there was an example of inappropriate following behaviour, where the model began to follow a leading vehicle into a cycle lane, indicating a need for improved scene understanding and lane boundary awareness.

Environmental factors may have influenced performance in certain scenarios. Sun glare potentially impacted stop line detection at a red light, and it was noted that vehicle dynamics, including passenger load, may affect model behaviour. Performance also appeared to vary slightly between runs on the same route, suggesting some inconsistency.

Overall, the Dragonfish-Enormous-Ivory model demonstrates a solid safety baseline but lacks the refinement, intuitiveness, and autonomy seen in more recent models. Key areas for improvement include lane selection, navigation integration, consistency across runs, and more robust interpretation of complex road layouts and signals.

column11: Route search- M1 junction 5 to HQ
column58:
Feedback on Dragonfish-Enormous-Ivory Model Performance (Junction 5 M1 to HQ Run)

During the run from Junction 5 of the M1 to HQ, which included a mix of highway and urban driving, the Dragonfish-Enormous-Ivory model demonstrated generally stable and capable performance, particularly in urban environments.

On the highway, the model settled at around 67 mph and was able to increase speed further with additional throttle input. Lane discipline was acceptable but could be improved. Lane changes, especially when overtaking, lacked urgency and commitment. The model would benefit from initiating lane changes earlier and carrying more speed through the manoeuvre to better match the flow of surrounding traffic. In comparison, the Copper model handles overtaking and lane positioning more effectively, with stronger timing and confidence.

A notable safety concern occurred at a zebra crossing, where the model failed to come to a stop despite a pedestrian clearly about to cross. This required intervention and highlights the need for improved pedestrian awareness and prioritisation in these scenarios.

Outside of this, the model performed well in urban settings. It handled general city driving competently, maintaining good control, positioning, and composure throughout the remainder of the route.

Overall, while the model shows solid baseline performance across both highway and urban driving, improvements are needed in overtaking behaviour, lane change execution, and pedestrian handling to reach a higher level of consistency and confidence.

Feedback on Copper Panda Exciting (IGPU Model) – TWR Candidate Demo Route

During testing on the TWR Candidate Demo Route, the Copper Panda Exciting model demonstrated both strengths and areas for improvement. On the positive side, the model successfully navigated the first two width restrictions, including the narrow 6-foot restriction and a second restriction complicated by a parked car. It also handled a very narrow road with ease. However, the model failed to navigate the third width restriction, which required a lane change due to a priority direction. Instead of shifting lanes, it headed toward the raised pavement, requiring intervention.

The model’s driving style was notably assertive—more so than would be ideal for most urban settings. It tended to maintain speed as pedestrians approached crossings, only braking at the last moment. This led to close calls, and interventions were required at both a zebra crossing and an uncontrolled crossing. Additionally, the model’s handling of speed bumps was harsh. It slowed to around 14 mph before the front wheels crossed, but then accelerated, causing a significant impact on the rear wheels and making the ride uncomfortable. This aggressive approach over bumps is not only unpleasant for passengers but could also lead to unnecessary wear on the vehicle’s suspension.

In summary, while the model handled narrow sections and initial width restrictions well, it struggled with a more complex lane-change scenario. Its assertiveness, while potentially suited to some driving styles, was too aggressive in urban settings, especially around crossings and speed bumps. Further refinement is needed to balance assertiveness with comfort and

Feedback on Ibex-Lime-Meritorious Model Performance

During testing on this route with vehicle FME40, the Ibex-Lime-Meritorious model showed mixed performance, with several areas requiring improvement, particularly in low-speed urban scenarios and traffic handling.

At the exit from Tilyard Road, the model failed to creep forward as expected, limiting visibility and confidence when joining the main road. It also demonstrated delayed reaction to external cues, specifically failing to respond promptly to flashing headlights from another vehicle indicating that it was giving way. This resulted in hesitation and reduced fluidity in what should have been a straightforward interaction.

In start-stop traffic conditions, the model’s behaviour was noticeably unrefined. It applied excessive throttle when moving off, followed by sharp and abrupt braking, leading to a jerky and uncomfortable experience within the cabin. This included an audible “thump” during braking, further highlighting the lack of smooth control. The model would benefit from a more gradual and controlled approach to low-speed movement, with improved “rolling” behaviour when creeping forward and a smoother ramp-down when coming to a stop.

A further concern was observed at a traffic light, where the model failed to remain stationary at a red signal for the forward lane while a separate left-turn-only light was green. This indicates a need for clearer interpretation of lane-specific traffic signals and improved compliance in multi-signal scenarios.

After clearing heavy traffic, the model’s performance improved. It handled the remainder of the route more competently and successfully navigated both width restrictions without issue, demonstrating capability in constrained environments.

Overall, while the model showed some strengths once out of dense traffic, its performance in key urban scenarios—particularly junction behaviour, interaction with other road users, and low-speed control—was not at a level suitable for a demo. Further refinement in these areas would be required before it can be confidently used in customer-facing demonstrations

Feedback on Ibex-Lime-Meritorious Model Performance (Vehicle FME20020)

During this run in vehicle FME20020, the Ibex-Lime-Meritorious model showed a clear improvement compared to previous experience in FME20040. The overall driving behaviour was noticeably smoother, particularly in low-speed and urban scenarios, resulting in a more comfortable and controlled experience.

The model demonstrated a good balance of assertiveness and smoothness when executing turns, handling them confidently without compromising passenger comfort. This was especially evident when navigating narrow roads with parked vehicles on both sides, as well as managing oncoming traffic. In these constrained environments, the model maintained a composed and assertive approach, which contributed to a more natural and human-like driving style.

Pedestrian handling was also improved. The model was able to identify pedestrians from a greater distance and responded appropriately by coming to a gentle and controlled stop, rather than exhibiting the previously observed abrupt or jerky braking behaviour. This added to the overall refinement and safety perception of the drive.

There was, however, a minor navigation-related issue during the run. The model deviated from the planned route and briefly navigated around a side square before rejoining the intended path. While this did not require intervention and the system recovered on its own, it suggests some inconsistency in route adherence that may need further investigation.

Overall, the model showed meaningful progress in smoothness, anticipation, and confidence. Compared to earlier runs, this version felt more suitable for real-world driving and demonstrated improved consistency in key urban scenarios.

column11: Short British museum, start and end at Britannia st
column58:
Feedback on Ibex-Lime-Meritorious Model Performance (Short British Museum Route – Balanced Mode)

During a 30-minute autonomous test on a shorter version of the British Museum route, starting and ending on Britannia Street, the Ibex-Lime-Meritorious model demonstrated a solid baseline performance across a range of complex urban scenarios. The run was conducted in balanced mode under overcast conditions with light drizzle, providing a realistic urban driving environment.

In pedestrian interactions, the model handled most situations well and showed good situational awareness. It reacted promptly when pedestrians made late decisions to cross, including a scenario where a couple initially hesitated before committing. In another instance, the model correctly judged uncertainty from pedestrians and proceeded appropriately. However, when a pedestrian approached a zebra crossing at speed, the model responded with a sudden stop. While the decision to stop was correct, the braking felt abrupt and could be refined for improved passenger comfort.

In general traffic behaviour, the model maintained safe and accurate following distances, allowing clear visibility of tyres and tarmac. While this is appropriate from a safety perspective, there were moments where slightly reducing the gap in heavier traffic could improve flow and prevent other vehicles from filling the space unnecessarily. At unprotected right turns, the model would benefit from edging forward more proactively as the final oncoming vehicles pass, allowing for a more efficient and confident completion of the manoeuvre.

Several technical behaviours were noted that would benefit from refinement. Indicator timing was occasionally delayed, which reduced the ability of surrounding traffic to anticipate the vehicle’s intentions and avoid blocking key lanes. Speed bump handling was also suboptimal, with the vehicle not positioning itself centrally over single bumps, leading to a less comfortable traversal compared to typical human driving behaviour. In one instance approaching an amber light at approximately 20 mph, the model reduced speed prematurely before accelerating again once it determined that stopping was not feasible. A more consistent approach based on distance to the stop line would improve decision-making in these scenarios.

Steering precision also showed minor inconsistencies. When turning right into Britannia Street, the model applied slightly excessive steering input and held it for too long before recentering. While it appeared to self-correct, the delay in adjustment reduced the overall smoothness of the manoeuvre.

Overall, the model performed well across this condensed urban route, successfully handling a range of pedestrian interactions, lane positioning requirements, width restrictions, and give-way scenarios. The main areas for improvement are centred around refinement of smoothness, earlier signalling, better anticipation in junction positioning, and more consistent decision-making in edge cases such as amber lights and pedestrian approaches. Addressing these points would further enhance both safety perception and passenger comfort.

column4: Free plot from Britain’s to HQ
column58:
Feedback on Compassionate-Songbird-Lavender Model Performance (Britannia Street to Wave HQ Route)

During a 16-minute test run from Britannia Street to Wave HQ, the Compassionate-Songbird-Lavender model demonstrated a noticeable improvement in driving behaviour compared to the baseline model. The overall experience felt more assertive yet controlled, with smoother and more progressive inputs that made the vehicle’s movements feel less robotic and more natural.

One of the key strengths of this model was its speed management. It consistently reached the 20 mph speed limit, unlike the baseline model which tended to remain below this threshold. This contributed to better integration with surrounding traffic and improved overall flow. The model also handled give-way scenarios effectively by creeping forward at low speed rather than coming to a complete stop, allowing it to maintain momentum and proceed smoothly once safe to do so. This behaviour was further reflected in its positioning, where it proactively edged forward to improve visibility instead of stopping and then moving off.

Following distance was well managed, maintaining a safe and practical gap where only the tyres of the vehicle ahead were visible, avoiding unnecessarily large spacing. The model also responded well to dynamic hazards, including a scenario involving an overspeeding van. It reduced speed significantly but in a controlled and smooth manner, then crept forward to allow the vehicle to pass safely before continuing.

In more complex scenarios, the model performed confidently. It successfully navigated a 5.5 width restriction without issue and handled a double roundabout scenario involving a cyclist with good awareness. The model adjusted its speed appropriately as the cyclist approached, correctly interpreted the cyclist’s path at the first roundabout, and continued safely once the situation was clear. Additionally, it demonstrated improved autonomy when entering the yard at the end of the route, completing the manoeuvre without requiring throttle assistance, unlike the baseline model.

Overall, the model presents a clear step forward in terms of smoothness, assertiveness, and natural driving behaviour. It feels more intuitive and capable across a range of scenarios. While further testing is required on more complex routes such as the British Museum route, and limitations such as reversing capability remain, the current performance indicates strong progress and improved real-world applicability.

Feedback on Effervescent-Lark-Emerald Model Performance (Turin, Italy – 04/05/2026, 06:55 Run)

During the early morning run on 04/05/2026 at 06:55, the Effervescent-Lark-Emerald model showed limitations in adapting to real-world traffic flow, particularly during the transition from Germany into Italy. While the model maintained a safe baseline, its overall driving behaviour lacked the confidence and responsiveness required for efficient motorway and urban integration.

On higher-speed roads, the model struggled with speed management. It was inconsistent in maintaining appropriate speeds, at times failing to reach or hold the speed limit, and in other instances being slow to accelerate when conditions clearly allowed for it. This impacted overall traffic flow and reduced confidence in its ability to match surrounding vehicles.

Overtaking behaviour also required improvement. The model was hesitant in taking opportunities, often delaying manoeuvres despite safe and clear conditions. In several cases, it waited for multiple vehicles to pass before initiating an overtake, which reduced efficiency and highlighted a lack of decisiveness.

Overall, while the model remained safe, it did not perform competitively when compared to other models tested. Improvements are needed in speed confidence, overtaking behaviour, and better alignment with real-world traffic dynamics, particularly in regions like Italy where assertiveness and adaptability are essential

Feedback on Effervescent-Lark-Emerald Model Performance (Turin, Italy – 05/05/2026, 08:38 Run)

During the run on 05/05/2026 at 08:38, the Effervescent-Lark-Emerald model continued to show challenges in handling Turin’s complex and unconventional road layouts, particularly in relation to lane selection and route adherence.

A key issue observed was incorrect lane positioning when preparing for turns. In one instance, the model attempted to make a right turn from a 50 kph lane where only straight movement was permitted. The correct behaviour would have been to position itself in the adjacent lane of the same direction, which operates at 30 kph and allows access to side roads. This highlights a gap in understanding local road structure and lane-specific rules.

More broadly, the model struggled with selecting the appropriate lane in urban environments, which impacted its ability to execute planned manoeuvres reliably. While it maintained a cautious approach at junctions, this was not sufficient to compensate for the lack of accuracy in positioning and planning.

In summary, the model continues to operate safely but lacks the precision and adaptability required for complex urban driving in Turin. Further development is needed in lane interpretation, navigation alignment, and handling of non-standard road layouts to improve overall performance and reliability.

column4: External Guest Demo - Tamaki Kawahira of CBRE, Naru Kitamura of CBRE、 5月12日(火) · 11:00 – 11:30

• Narrow Road Navigation:

• Speed & UI Discrepancy:

• Maneuver & Indicator Issues:
• Signal Failure: One lane change was executed without activating the turn signal.
• Yielding Error: In one instance, the vehicle advanced too far despite an oncoming vehicle approaching.

Feedback on Compassionate-Songbird-Lavender Model Performance (DWR Route – 08:43 Run)

During testing on the DWR route at 08:43, the Compassionate-Songbird-Lavender model demonstrated strong situational awareness and smooth, controlled behaviour across a range of complex urban scenarios. The overall driving style felt natural and composed, with good anticipation and confidence in constrained environments.

One of the most notable moments was at the narrow entrance on Tillyard Road, where two parked vans and an oncoming Uber created a highly restricted space. The model handled this scenario particularly well, correctly identifying the need to mount the pavement corner slightly with the rear wheel in order to complete the manoeuvre safely. This showed a high level of spatial awareness and practical decision-making.

Pedestrian awareness was another strong point. At a zebra crossing where visibility was partially obstructed by a truck, the model maintained a cautious approach, holding back appropriately. Its behaviour adjusted smoothly once the situation became clearer, demonstrating a well-balanced response to uncertainty. Overall, interactions with pedestrians felt measured and confident.

The model also showed strong fundamentals in general driving behaviour. Early indication was a standout feature, with signals given well in advance, allowing surrounding traffic to react naturally and improving overall flow. Following distance was well judged, maintaining a safe and practical gap, and deceleration into stops felt smooth and controlled. It also handled emerging vehicles effectively, including a van entering from a one-way system, where it balanced caution with gradual forward movement. Decision-making at unprotected right turns was generally good, with the model able to take appropriate gaps in traffic.

There are, however, areas that would benefit from refinement. Lane positioning on Acton Street was not optimal, particularly when preparing for a left turn. The model remained too central in the road rather than merging left earlier, which resulted in unnecessary disruption to traffic flow. Additionally, while safe, acceleration through unprotected turns could be more assertive. In one instance, manual throttle input was required to complete a turn efficiently, highlighting a need for more decisive execution in these scenarios.

Vehicle-specific performance differences were also observed. On FME2020, the model previously handled all three width restrictions on the DWR route without issue. However, on FME2040, it failed the second width restriction multiple times, only succeeding after an indicator prompt was provided. The model also felt noticeably less smooth on FME2040, with more jerky behaviour compared to the more refined performance experienced on FME2020. Despite this, some behaviours remained consistent, such as correctly stopping at the closed HQ gate with the indicator on and waiting appropriately for it to open.

Overall, the Compassionate-Songbird-Lavender model shows strong capability, particularly in awareness, smoothness, and early communication with other road users. Further improvements in lane positioning, assertiveness in key manoeuvres, and consistency across vehicles would help elevate its performance to a higher level of reliability and confidence.

Feedback on Compassionate-Songbird-Lavender Model Performance (Short British Museum Route – 14:25 Run)

During the 14:25 test session on the shortened British Museum route starting from Britannia Street, the Compassionate-Songbird-Lavender model demonstrated a generally smooth and capable driving style, though some regression was observed in pedestrian interaction compared to earlier runs and recent demos.

In terms of route execution, the model performed well. It successfully navigated all width restrictions, showing improvement from the morning test where it had previously failed on the second restriction. The vehicle handled the double roundabout smoothly and maintained good composure on narrower roads, particularly along Guildford Street. It also demonstrated appropriate judgement during an unprotected right turn involving an oncoming cyclist, managing the interaction safely and confidently.

However, pedestrian handling presented some concerns. The model encountered multiple scenarios involving jaywalkers, where it appeared to assess pedestrian intent but delayed its final decision-making. This resulted in late braking responses, requiring intervention in at least one instance where the vehicle stopped too close to crossing pedestrians. While the model ultimately reacted correctly, the timing of these decisions reduced overall safety perception. On a more positive note, it handled a cyclist emerging from the pavement well, responding immediately with a controlled stop.

From a system perspective, there were UI-related issues during the run. Time and arrival estimates remained fixed at approximately 40 minutes from the start of the route and only updated midway through the journey. Similarly, the distance display corrected itself partway through, changing from 6.7 miles to 2 miles. Despite this, map tracking and GPS positioning remained accurate throughout.

It is worth noting that external conditions may have influenced performance. Foot traffic was noticeably heavier than in previous tests, which likely contributed to the increased complexity of pedestrian interactions. Additionally, the vehicle had been operating continuously without a restart in warmer conditions, which may have had some impact on system behaviour.

Overall, the model felt smoother than the baseline in vehicle FME2040 and performed well in route navigation and general driving behaviour. However, improvements are needed in pedestrian decision timing and consistency to ensure confidence in more dynamic, high-footfall environments, particularly in preparation for demo scenarios.

Feedback on Compassionate-Songbird-Lavender Model Performance – 14:15 VIP Demo

During the 14:15 VIP demo, using the same route as the earlier 12:30 run, the Compassionate-Songbird-Lavender model maintained a good overall level of performance. The vehicle continued to handle urban driving scenarios effectively, responding correctly to road regulations, traffic controls, zebra crossings, and dynamic traffic conditions.

The overall driving experience remained smooth and capable, with similar observations to the earlier runs regarding comfort over speed bumps and general refinement. Nothing unusual stood out in terms of driving behaviour or decision-making.

However, the most significant issue during this run was the number of uncommanded disengagements encountered. A total of four occurred throughout the demo, with two specifically happening during turns. The disengagements did not appear to be linked to speed bumps, potholes, or obvious road surface disturbances, suggesting the possibility of a software-related issue rather than an environmental trigger.

Despite these interruptions, the model continued to demonstrate strong baseline driving capability and good adaptability to urban driving conditions overall.

Feedback on Compassionate-Songbird-Lavender Model Performance – 12:30 Free Plot Demo

During the 12:30 demo on a different free plot route lasting approximately 27 minutes, the Compassionate-Songbird-Lavender model delivered one of its strongest performances of the day. The drive felt smooth, natural, and highly capable across a range of urban scenarios, with the model continuing to respond appropriately to traffic controls, pedestrian crossings, amber lights, and keep clear zones.

Overall, the run felt close to flawless in terms of driving behaviour and confidence. The model handled traffic well and maintained good composure throughout the route. The only noticeable areas for improvement related to comfort over speed bumps, where the vehicle could still reduce speed further and align itself more effectively to improve cabin comfort during traversal.

There was also one uncommanded disengagement during the run, again without a clear environmental explanation. Outside of this isolated event, the model performed to a very high standard.

Feedback on Compassionate-Songbird-Lavender Model Performance – 11:00 Family & Friends Demo

During the 11:00 Family & Friends demo on the 25-minute free plot route, the Compassionate-Songbird-Lavender model performed well overall, demonstrating strong compliance with road regulations and good adaptability to unexpected road scenarios. The model handled amber and red lights appropriately, responded correctly to zebra crossings and keep clear zones, and maintained a generally smooth and confident driving style throughout the run.

One particularly positive scenario involved a jaywalker who appeared uncertain about where they intended to go. The model reacted well by significantly reducing speed without coming to a complete stop, while also remaining composed when the pedestrian suddenly changed direction toward the front side of the vehicle. This interaction felt natural and controlled, showing good awareness and measured decision-making.

There are still areas where refinement would improve passenger comfort. The model could benefit from reducing speed further over speed bumps, as the current approach still feels slightly too aggressive within the cabin. Additionally, there was one uncommanded disengagement during the run without any obvious environmental trigger or clear reason for the event.

Overall, the run was successful and demonstrated a strong level of maturity in general urban driving behaviour.

Feedback on Cosmic-Crane-Chocolate Model Performance (09:31 London Test Route)

During testing beginning at 09:31 across a variety of London driving scenarios, the Cosmic-Crane-Chocolate model demonstrated a strong overall baseline, particularly in speed compliance, smooth braking, and general spatial awareness. The model consistently reached and maintained the 20 mph speed limit when conditions allowed, which made its behaviour feel more natural and better integrated with surrounding traffic compared to models that tend to remain below the limit unnecessarily.

Braking performance was another standout area. Deceleration into red lights and stops felt smooth and progressive, avoiding the sharp or “startled” braking behaviour seen in some previous models. The model also demonstrated good environmental awareness, successfully adjusting position to provide space for a driver opening a car door and confidently navigating multiple width restrictions, including 7’0 and 6’6 restrictions, at sensible speeds.

Pedestrian interaction was generally well handled. At zebra crossings, the model judged pedestrian timing accurately, including a scenario involving a woman with a pram where it came to a smooth and controlled stop. It also reacted appropriately to jaywalkers, stopping when necessary while correctly continuing when pedestrians hesitated or held back. In narrow London streets, the model showed a welcome level of assertiveness, helping maintain flow in environments where hesitant behaviour can create unnecessary disruption.

There were, however, several areas where decision-making and progression could be improved. At Tillyard Road, the model failed to creep forward at a give-way junction to improve visibility and remained static for an extended period without showing intent to proceed. In another scenario involving a vehicle waiting to perform an unprotected right turn, the model missed a safe opportunity to progress through a gap on the left and instead waited for the entire manoeuvre to complete.

Lane positioning and timing also require refinement. On Acton Street, the model delayed a lane change until midway through the road despite having sufficient opportunity to position earlier. While not technically incorrect, this behaviour reduced overall traffic flow and appeared less natural. Similarly, at an amber traffic light, the model hesitated between stopping and continuing, ultimately braking while crossing the primary stop line, where a more decisive continuation through the junction would have been preferable.

Road closure handling exposed more significant navigation issues. Although the model initially rerouted appropriately, it later attempted to position itself incorrectly on Swinton Street by indicating left and moving toward a lane designated for right turns only. The system appeared not to correctly interpret painted road markings or traffic light instructions prohibiting left turns. Following manual intervention to return to the planned route, the model then made a poor priority decision near a zebra crossing, yielding unnecessarily to a cyclist despite having priority on the main road. This resulted in the vehicle stopping partially over the crossing area while surrounding traffic began to move.

Comfort and refinement over speed bumps could also be improved. The model currently traverses them at approximately 14 mph, whereas closer to 10 mph would provide a more comfortable experience for passengers, especially over isolated speed bumps. That said, the vehicle did attempt a more balanced approach over bumps compared to other models, resulting in less cabin disturbance overall. Enhanced recognition of different speed bump types and road surface changes would likely improve comfort further.

Overall, the Cosmic-Crane-Chocolate model delivered a capable and confident performance across a complex London route, successfully handling width restrictions, pedestrian interactions, and general urban driving. Its strongest qualities were speed management, smooth braking, and spatial awareness. The main areas for development relate to progression, lane change anticipation, navigation interpretation, and more decisive handling of ambiguous situations.

Feedback on Zebra-Striped-Fuchsia Model Performance

During this test run, the Zebra-Striped-Fuchsia model showed noticeably over-cautious behaviour compared to other demo baseline models, particularly in traffic interaction and decision-making at junctions.

One of the main issues observed was at intersections and unprotected turns. When exiting the HQ yard, the model stopped abruptly and positioned itself to the right instead of continuing normally when a vehicle approached from the left. In another scenario involving an unprotected right turn, the model initially behaved correctly by edging forward while a truck was indicating left. However, when a cyclist appeared behind the truck, the vehicle unnecessarily steered left instead of simply slowing down and maintaining its path. This created awkward positioning and reduced confidence in the manoeuvre.

The model also struggled significantly with gap utilisation. Throughout the run, it consistently failed to take clear and safe opportunities to proceed through traffic gaps, often requiring throttle override to continue. Even after intervention, the vehicle would brake sharply once throttle input was released, despite there still being sufficient time and space to complete the manoeuvre safely. The behaviour gave the impression that the model was attempting to wait for every visible vehicle to fully clear before proceeding, regardless of distance or actual risk.

A major navigation issue also occurred during the route. The model incorrectly turned right instead of following the planned left turn and then attempted to enter a closed-off area. This may have been caused by misinterpreting a road closure sign or by a more fundamental routing issue. The closure only applied to traffic heading toward King’s Cross, whereas the intended route toward HQ remained open.

There were also several UI-related issues throughout the run. Time-based metrics appeared frozen from the start of the drive, with total journey time remaining at 24 minutes, ETA failing to update, and mileage not progressing correctly. Despite this, Mapbox navigation and the current MPH display continued functioning normally.

Another concern involved cyclist interaction at a give-way section. The model hesitated while assessing an approaching cyclist, then proceeded but stopped directly within the bike lane while the cyclist was still approaching. Throttle override was required to clear the lane safely, but once released, the model again braked sharply despite the surrounding situation.

The run concluded at approximately 09:25. Overall, while the model maintained a safe baseline, improvements are needed in confidence, smoother progression through gaps, cyclist interaction, and navigation reliability to make the driving behaviour feel more natural and suitable for demo environments.

Feedback on Baseline IPEX Model Performance (DWR Route)

During testing on the DWR route, the Baseline IPEX model demonstrated a more assertive driving style compared to several other models, particularly in its willingness to reach and maintain the speed limit when conditions allowed. This gave the vehicle a more confident feel in general traffic flow and helped avoid some of the hesitation seen in more cautious models.

The model handled the route reasonably well overall and successfully navigated both width restrictions. At the second restriction, it demonstrated good assertive behaviour when interacting with an oncoming vehicle, positioning itself confidently enough to indicate that the opposing driver should reverse. This behaviour felt natural and effective for London-style driving. Another standout moment occurred on Caledonian Road involving a jaywalker, where the model made a good judgement call by slightly increasing speed to safely pass through the situation rather than braking unnecessarily.

Despite these positives, several areas require refinement. The model consistently failed to creep forward at junctions and give-way scenarios to improve visibility, reducing confidence and progression in tighter urban environments. A notable example was on Tillyard Road, where the vehicle remained stationary behind a speed bump rather than edging forward naturally for better road visibility.

Steering behaviour also showed some inconsistencies. During a left turn, the model applied excessive steering input and was slow to recenter afterward, creating an oversteer effect that reduced smoothness. Braking performance was another area needing improvement, as deceleration often occurred in stages rather than as one progressive and controlled movement, making the ride feel jerky within the cabin.

Speed bump handling also requires refinement. While the model slowed appropriately on approach, it tended to accelerate before the rear tyres had fully cleared the bump, resulting in a harsh impact and unnecessary cabin movement. A more balanced approach throughout the entire traversal would improve both comfort and vehicle refinement.

There was also a navigation-related issue during the run where the model attempted to take an unplotted turn, preparing to turn right instead of following the planned left turn route. This required correction and highlights the need for improved navigation alignment and route interpretation.

Compared to other models tested, the Baseline IPEX model sits somewhere between the cautious behaviour of Zebra-Striped-Fuchsia and the more refined junction behaviour of Compassionate-Songbird-Lavender. While Zebra-Striped-Fuchsia often felt overly hesitant and missed safe opportunities in traffic, Compassionate-Songbird-Lavender demonstrated stronger positioning and creeping behaviour at give-ways. Ideally, combining the assertiveness of IPEX with the smoothness and anticipation of the stronger-performing models would create a more balanced and natural driving experience.

Overall, the Baseline IPEX model showed promising assertiveness and traffic integration but would benefit from smoother control inputs, improved junction positioning, better navigation reliability, and more refined comfort behaviour to achieve a more polished overall performance

Feedback on Wallaby-Compact-Moccasin Model Performance (DWR Route)

During testing on the DWR route, the Wallaby-Compact-Moccasin model demonstrated a calmer and less assertive driving style compared to the Ibex model. The vehicle accelerated progressively and comfortably, although it generally remained limited to around 19 mph rather than reaching the full 20 mph speed limit. Overall, the driving behaviour felt slower and more cautious, which created a smoother experience in some situations but reduced confidence in more dynamic traffic scenarios.

One of the stronger aspects of the model was its navigation reliability. Unlike several other models tested, including Ibex, Zebra, and Compassionate-Songbird-Lavender, the Wallaby model did not experience navigation failures or incorrect turn indications during the route. Route following remained consistent throughout the drive, which made the overall experience feel more dependable. The model also handled both width restrictions successfully without requiring intervention.

The model showed some positive traffic interaction behaviours, including early indication in certain scenarios, which gave surrounding vehicles more time to react and reposition naturally. However, there were still some lane management issues. On Caledonian Road, the vehicle selected the left lane instead of the middle lane for an upcoming left turn, which then created a more difficult merge situation for the following right turn onto Kings Cross Road. Similarly, on Acton Street, lane changes were delayed despite earlier opportunities being available, although this behaviour is shared with several other models on the same road.

There were also several safety and refinement concerns observed during the run. At a give-way section, the model failed to proceed even after another driver flashed headlights to clearly give priority. Despite surrounding traffic slowing appropriately, the vehicle remained hesitant and required assistance to continue. In comparison, models such as Ibex and Compassionate-Songbird-Lavender reacted much more naturally in similar situations.

Pedestrian handling around zebra crossings also requires improvement. In one scenario where a truck blocked visibility near a crossing, the model failed to reduce speed sufficiently, requiring manual intervention to avoid a potentially unsafe interaction with a pedestrian. Speed bump behaviour was also inconsistent, as the vehicle occasionally accelerated before the rear tyres had fully cleared the bump, causing an uncomfortable impact through the cabin floor.

Overall, the Wallaby-Compact-Moccasin model feels suitable for demo scenarios when collaborative driving features are available, as it maintains a calm and predictable driving style. Its strongest advantage over some other models is navigation reliability and route consistency. However, it still requires improvement in assertiveness, progression through give-way situations, pedestrian anticipation, and comfort refinement to deliver a more natural and confident driving experience.
