Feedback on Racoon-Ecstatic-Amaranth Model Performance – British Museum Route Testing

Positive Performance Observations

The Racoon-Ecstatic-Amaranth model demonstrated several strong qualities throughout the British Museum route testing. It consistently reached the 20 mph speed limit smoothly and maintained good lane positioning during most urban driving scenarios. Confidence through narrow London roads was also impressive, with the vehicle navigating tight gaps naturally and without unnecessary hesitation.

Pedestrian awareness was another clear strength. The model identified pedestrians early at zebra crossings and responded with gentle, progressive braking rather than abrupt stops, creating a more comfortable and reassuring experience within the cabin. The vehicle also showed good anticipation approaching traffic lights by changing lanes proactively, helping avoid blocking traffic in middle lanes.

General road etiquette was positive overall, with the model allowing non-priority vehicles to merge appropriately when safe to do so. In addition, it successfully navigated multiple width restrictions while operating in Bolt mode, maintaining good control and positioning throughout these tighter sections.

⸻

Areas Requiring Improvement

Despite the strong baseline performance, several technical and refinement issues became apparent during the test route.

At the Tillyard Road give-way section, the model became overly hesitant and failed to creep forward naturally to improve visibility, requiring manual override to encourage progression. Steering behaviour also lacked refinement at lower speeds, with noticeable twitching and repeated corrections, particularly when recentering toward the right.

The most significant issue was the vehicle’s behaviour in stop-start traffic across all modes tested, including Balanced, Calm, and Bolt. The model repeatedly applied excessive throttle when moving off, followed by sharp braking shortly afterward. This created a jerky and uncomfortable experience for passengers and reduced overall smoothness significantly. Switching between modes did not meaningfully improve the behaviour, and Bolt mode introduced even more aggressive steering corrections.

Speed bump handling also requires major improvement. In one scenario, the vehicle crossed a significant speed bump at approximately 40 mph, resulting in excessive cabin disturbance and poor comfort. Cyclist interaction was another concern, as the model initiated an overtake too close to a cyclist near a traffic island, reducing safe clearance and confidence in the manoeuvre.

⸻

Overall Demo Assessment

While the model demonstrated strong navigation capability, pedestrian awareness, lane discipline, and confidence through narrow urban roads, the comfort and smoothness issues in slow-moving traffic significantly affected the overall experience. Due to the high amount of stop-start traffic on this route, the model was ultimately deemed unsuitable for the planned 11 AM demo, and an alternative model was selected for the return journey to base

column4: Testing a model on the CLA
column58:
Mercedes CLA – Autonomous Model Feedback Form

Test Information

Model Tested: Influential-manta-ray-magenta
Route: SWR V2 (25-minute route)
Test Type: First-time demo reconnaissance testing

The session focused on evaluating overall autonomous driving behaviour, particularly throttle response and DILC (Driver Initiated Lane Change) functionality. The selected route provided a good range of dynamic urban scenarios suitable for assessing model performance.

⸻

General Performance Summary

The model demonstrated a mixed level of performance throughout the route. While several manoeuvres were handled confidently and smoothly, the overall behaviour is not yet at a level suitable for demo purposes. The biggest concerns were related to lane positioning and hesitation at junctions, both of which would likely generate negative feedback during external demonstrations.

Overall performance can best be described as “not great, not bad,” but significant refinement and additional training are still required before the model is demo-ready.

⸻

Key Issues Observed

Lane Positioning

The most consistent issue throughout the drive was poor lane positioning. The vehicle repeatedly drove too far to the right, frequently tracking close to or directly on yellow and double-yellow lines.

In tighter sections of road, the model failed to move left adequately for oncoming traffic and behaved as though it was compensating for a vehicle larger than the actual CLA footprint. One notable example occurred on a downhill section where the vehicle nearly crossed onto double red lines.

This behaviour would be highly noticeable in a customer or stakeholder demo environment and currently raises concerns around confidence and spatial awareness.

⸻

Junction Behaviour and Acceleration

The model struggled significantly when moving off from giveway and T-junction scenarios.

Three separate interventions were required while exiting Tilliard Road due to the vehicle failing to accelerate and commit to available gaps. At several junctions, the system appeared overly cautious, hesitating despite safe opportunities to proceed.

The behaviour reduced traffic flow confidence and would likely frustrate passengers or surrounding road users during a live demo.

⸻

Uncommanded Disengagement

One unexpected disengagement occurred at a width restriction point during the route. Further investigation may be required to understand the trigger condition.

⸻

Positive Observations

Despite the issues above, there were several areas where the model performed well.

The vehicle handled unprotected right turns confidently and demonstrated good judgement in gap assessment. It also successfully navigated the 6x6 width restriction without issue and correctly kept a yellow box junction clear despite a misleading traffic gap ahead.

Additional positives included:

* Smooth continuation through an amber traffic light without unnecessary hesitation
* Good handling of a downhill tight right-hand turn
* Appropriate response to an open car door scenario by moving left safely
* Reliable navigation performance throughout the route, especially compared with recent Mach-E navigation behaviour

⸻

Safety Intervention

A precautionary intervention was required at a zebra crossing involving school children and a teacher waiting at the roadside.

Although the model may have been interpreting the teacher’s body language as non-crossing intent, the vehicle did not sufficiently reduce speed approaching the crossing. The intervention was precautionary rather than safety-critical, but the behaviour highlighted the need for more conservative decision-making around vulnerable road users and child-related scenarios.

⸻

Final Assessment

At its current level of performance, the model is not ready for customer-facing or stakeholder demo activity.

The primary blockers are:

* Inconsistent lane positioning
* Excessive hesitation at junctions
* Confidence issues in tighter road environments

While some individual manoeuvres were handled very well, the overall drive quality still lacks the consistency expected for demo readiness.

Additionally, unlike the Mach-E models, the CLA currently does not support automatic return-to-yard functionality

* There were instances at blind intersections and busy junctions where the vehicle would not proceed without manual throttle input.
* During right turns, there were cases where the vehicle would not merge right unless the indicator was manually activated.
* The vehicle attempted to change into the left lane despite parked vehicles being present on the left side.
* At the same location as previously observed, the vehicle performed lane changes within the intersection and failed to activate the indicator.
* Additionally, there was one instance where the lane change execution was slightly unstable.

Feedback on Green-Imaginative-Ram Model Performance (Licensing Route)

Overall Performance

During testing on the Licensing Route, the Green-Imaginative-Ram model delivered a generally solid performance and completed the full route without requiring any interventions. The driving behaviour felt assertive without becoming overly aggressive, allowing the vehicle to integrate reasonably well with surrounding traffic while maintaining a safe and controlled driving style.

Although the overall capability of the model was positive, the current braking behaviour prevents it from being considered fully demo-ready at this stage.

⸻

Positive Performance Observations

The model handled general speed management well, maintaining consistent urban speeds around 19 mph and operating steadily on highways at approximately 67–68 mph. While it did not consistently reach the full 70 mph limit, motorway driving felt controlled and stable, particularly during lane changes and merging scenarios.

Lane discipline on highways was good overall, with the model correctly using indicators and returning to default lanes after overtaking slower traffic. It also handled more dynamic environments effectively, including construction zones and temporary traffic light systems.

One notable strength compared to other models was the smoother throttle behaviour in stop-start traffic. Acceleration felt more progressive and less aggressive, resulting in a more comfortable cabin experience. The model also performed well when overtaking stationary vehicles and reacted appropriately during a jaywalker interaction later in the route.

⸻

Areas Requiring Improvement

The most significant issue observed during testing was unnecessary harsh braking behaviour. On two occasions, the model applied sharp braking where smoother speed management would have been more appropriate. One example occurred behind a van waiting at traffic lights, where the vehicle accelerated unnecessarily before braking firmly instead of maintaining a slower rolling approach. A similar issue occurred approaching a blocked yellow box junction.

Pedestrian prioritisation also requires improvement. In one scenario at a zebra crossing, the model failed to reduce speed appropriately for a pedestrian waiting to cross, appearing to prioritise vehicle flow instead of preparing to give way. Compared to stronger-performing models, this behaviour felt less natural and less cautious around pedestrian interactions.

Speed bump handling could also be refined further. The vehicle currently travels over bumps too quickly and would benefit from slowing to approximately 13–14 mph before progressively accelerating afterward to improve cabin comfort and reduce harsh impacts.

At lower speeds, some steering instability was noticeable, with slight steering wheel twitching occurring around 8 mph. Additional lane positioning issues appeared in areas where road markings were faded, particularly near larger vehicles such as trucks. There were also some incorrect lane selections for upcoming turns, where the model positioned itself in the wrong lane despite clearer alternatives being available.

⸻

Overall Assessment

Overall, the Green-Imaginative-Ram model demonstrates a capable and confident baseline with good highway behaviour, smooth throttle application in stop-start traffic, and strong general traffic integration. However, the harsh braking incidents, pedestrian prioritisation concerns, and low-speed refinement issues currently reduce passenger comfort and overall confidence.

While the model shows strong potential and completed the Licensing Route without intervention, further refinement in braking smoothness, pedestrian anticipation, and lane positioning would be required before it is suitable for customer-facing demo environments

Feedback on Green-Imaginative-Ram Model Performance – Urban Test

Overall Performance

During the urban test session, the Green-Imaginative-Ram model demonstrated a generally capable and confident driving style. The vehicle completed the full route without requiring intervention and overall felt assertive without becoming overly aggressive. While the baseline capability was solid, several refinement issues, particularly around braking smoothness, currently prevent the model from being considered demo-ready.

⸻

Speed & Traffic Management

The model maintained consistent urban speeds of approximately 19 mph and generally handled speed limit transitions appropriately, although the UI occasionally delayed updating displayed speed limits. On highways, the vehicle tended to stabilise around 67–68 mph rather than progressing fully to 70 mph.

Motorway lane changes and merging behaviour were handled confidently, with correct use of indicators and appropriate return to default lanes after overtaking slower vehicles. In urban traffic, throttle behaviour in stop-start conditions felt smoother and less aggressive compared to several other models tested.

The model also demonstrated good progression through temporary road layouts, including construction zones and temporary traffic light systems. Overtaking of stationary vehicles was completed confidently, and the vehicle reacted appropriately during a jaywalker scenario observed at approximately 14:17.

⸻

Critical Issues Identified

The most significant concern throughout the test was braking refinement. At approximately 14:32, while approaching a van waiting at traffic lights, the model accelerated unnecessarily before applying a harsh braking input instead of maintaining a slower rolling approach. A similar behaviour occurred later approaching a blocked yellow box junction, where throttle application followed by abrupt braking reduced comfort and smoothness within the cabin.

Pedestrian prioritisation also requires improvement. At approximately 14:02, the model failed to reduce speed appropriately for a pedestrian waiting at a zebra crossing, appearing to prioritise surrounding vehicle flow over pedestrian intent. Compared to stronger-performing models, this interaction felt less natural and less cautious.

⸻

Technical & Comfort Observations

Speed bump handling remains too aggressive. The model would benefit from reducing speed to approximately 13–14 mph before progressively accelerating once the vehicle has fully cleared the bump. Current behaviour creates unnecessary cabin movement and discomfort.

At lower speeds, slight steering wheel twitching was noticeable around approximately 8 mph, particularly during minor corrections. Additional lane positioning inconsistencies were observed in areas with faded road markings, especially when travelling alongside larger vehicles such as trucks.

There were also several lane selection issues for upcoming turns, where the model occasionally selected the incorrect lane despite clearer positioning opportunities being available.

⸻

Overall Assessment & Next Steps

Overall, the Green-Imaginative-Ram model demonstrates strong potential with good traffic integration, confident highway behaviour, and smoother throttle application in urban stop-start traffic compared to some other models. However, harsh braking patterns, pedestrian prioritisation concerns, and low-speed refinement issues currently impact passenger comfort and confidence.

At this stage, the model is not recommended for demo use until braking refinement has improved. Further evaluation will continue with additional testing planned on the SW route, with particular focus on braking behaviour consistency and urban interaction refinement.

Feedback on Zebra-Striped-Fuchsia Model Performance – SWR V2 Demo Route (09:15–10:15)

During the SWR V2 demo route between 09:15 and 10:15, the Zebra-Striped-Fuchsia model initially behaved as expected and showed normal baseline driving behaviour. However, performance became increasingly inconsistent once the route reached more complex negotiation scenarios, particularly around Brewery Road where traffic interaction is often challenging.

A key issue occurred when a taxi blocked the vehicle from proceeding ahead. Instead of repositioning itself further to the left to free space and help maintain traffic flow, the model appeared to become stuck in the situation. A more natural response would have been to adjust road positioning proactively to allow better negotiation with surrounding traffic.

Shortly afterward, the model incorrectly began indicating left toward an unplanned route on two separate occasions. These situations were overcome using over-throttle input. The most significant issue then occurred when the vehicle failed to execute a right turn despite Driver Intervention Lane Change (DILC) being active. The model instead proceeded forward and then attempted to turn right into an oncoming one-way lane, resulting in a required intervention. This was particularly concerning given that the route is a well-known local demo route that has been completed many times previously.

Following the intervention, AV was re-engaged while off-route after the UI had rerouted the navigation. The vehicle then continued until encountering a stationary bus with hazard lights activated. In this scenario, the model required DILC assistance to proceed around the obstruction, which is unusual for this area and not typically required with stronger-performing models.

Overall, the drive felt highly inconsistent compared to previous experiences on the same route. The unexpected navigation decisions, poor negotiation behaviour, and unreliable route handling significantly reduced confidence in the model’s stability. Based on this experience, the current behaviour of Zebra-Striped-Fuchsia, along with several of the newer models showing similar unpredictability, does not feel suitable or reliable enough for customer-facing demos at this time.

Feedback on Peach-Partridge-Healthy Model Performance – SWR V2 Route (11:30 Run)

On the 11:30 run of the SWR V2 route, the Peach-Partridge-Healthy model performed well. Overall, the model was assertive—bordering on aggressive—but never felt unsafe. It made no critical mistakes on lane positioning, zebra crossings, or general traffic adherence. The model slowed smoothly at crossings, behind stationary traffic, and over speed bumps, although it could slow more for bumps—similar to other models. Notably, it navigated the tight 6.6 width restriction confidently, slightly faster than a human but showcasing its capability. Both runs (including the upcoming 12:00) were family and friends demos, and this run had no major issues.

Feedback on Peach-Partridge-Healthy Model Performance – SWR V2 Route (12:00 Run)

The 12:00 run was consistent with the previous one. The model again showed assertiveness while remaining controlled. It performed well in all key areas, from lane discipline to smooth stopping at zebra crossings and behind stationary vehicles. On this run, a unique scenario arose near York Way: a man operating machinery blocked the path. The model stopped, though it should have slowed earlier to let him finish. Without disengaging AV, the vehicle was reversed using over-throttle, which impressed passengers. Overall, this run matched the previous—assertive, capable, and reliable. While it may be slightly too assertive for VIP demos, switching to candidate

Feedback on Wallaby-Compact-Moccasin Model Performance – SWR V2 Route

During testing on the SWR V2 route, the Wallaby-Compact-Moccasin model demonstrated generally good baseline driving behaviour, particularly in normal urban road scenarios. Lane positioning was balanced and consistent, with the model indicating early, taking turns at appropriate speeds, and maintaining good positioning within lanes. The vehicle also successfully unparked from a standstill and navigated independently to the Tilliard Road starting point with minimal throttle assistance, showing good low-speed confidence.

Throughout the route, however, several interventions were required due to hesitation and positioning issues. The most noticeable pattern was at give-way junctions, where the model repeatedly held back despite having safe opportunities to merge into traffic. At the Tilliard Road exit, for example, a truck created a large enough gap to proceed safely, but the model hesitated unnecessarily. This behaviour appeared consistently when transitioning from side roads onto busier main roads.

The model also struggled at the 6.6 width restriction. It approached the restriction extremely slowly and incorrectly steered toward the island, requiring intervention to prevent the rear wheel from clipping the curb. Compared to stronger-performing models such as Zebra and Peach, the Wallaby model lacked confidence and fluidity through these tighter sections.

Another intervention occurred on Brewery Road during an unprotected right-turn scenario. The model attempted to position itself ahead of a waiting van despite there not being enough available space. This would likely have blocked both the van’s intended movement and surrounding traffic flow. A more appropriate behaviour would have been to remain positioned behind the van while indicating for the turn.

There were also several positive behaviours observed during the route. The model made a correct and safe decision at a traffic light by remaining stopped after passing the primary stop line, rather than attempting to proceed unsafely through the junction. It also showed good judgment during an unprotected right turn involving an oncoming van and a pedestrian, choosing to continue the manoeuvre instead of hesitating and causing unnecessary traffic blockage.

Speed bump handling showed mixed results. The model positioned itself well over single speed bumps, distributing the vehicle evenly and reducing harsh cabin movement in a similar manner to the Ibex model. However, it still accelerated before the rear wheels had fully cleared the bump, creating an uncomfortable impact within the cabin. Full-width speed bumps were also taken too quickly overall, reducing passenger comfort.

Pedestrian interaction timing could also be improved. The model tended to become overly hesitant when pedestrians were nearby, leading to longer wait times and reduced traffic flow. Additionally, during unprotected right turns, the vehicle would benefit from positioning onto the unraised central reservation where appropriate, helping maintain smoother traffic progression.

Overall, all interventions during the run were non-safety critical but highlighted important areas for refinement. The Wallaby-Compact-Moccasin model demonstrates a solid baseline with good lane discipline and calm driving behaviour, but further improvement is needed in assertiveness, junction progression, width restriction confidence, and low-speed comfort refinement before it reaches stronger demo-ready standards.

Feedback on Cryptic-Lime-Marlin Model Performance – SWR V2 Route

During testing on the SWR V2 route, the Cryptic-Lime-Marlin model demonstrated a mixture of improvements and limitations when compared against the Wallaby baseline model. The testing primarily focused on unparking behaviour, junction navigation, and overall speed and positioning refinement in urban driving conditions.

One of the most significant issues occurred at the beginning of the run during an unparking scenario. The model failed to initiate movement despite throttle input and multiple attempts using indicators, requiring intervention before the route could properly begin. Additional intervention was later required at an unprotected right turn, where the vehicle positioned itself too early within the junction. Had the manoeuvre continued, it would likely have blocked oncoming traffic. The more appropriate behaviour would have been to remain behind the oncoming vehicle that was indicating right before completing the turn afterward.

Another positioning issue was observed near a zebra crossing, where the vehicle stopped with its rear tyres still partially over the crossing area rather than correctly positioning itself between the yellow box junction and the crossing. The model should have gradually crept forward to achieve a cleaner and safer stopping position.

Despite these concerns, the model showed several improvements compared to Wallaby. Speed bump handling was noticeably more refined, with smoother traversal and no aggressive acceleration while the rear tyres were still clearing the bump. Cabin comfort over both single and full-width speed bumps felt significantly improved overall.

The model also performed better at the 6.6 width restriction, successfully navigating through without rear tyre contact on the separation island, which had been an issue for the Wallaby model. Single speed bump positioning was also more balanced, resulting in a more comfortable experience for passengers.

One particularly positive behaviour involved junction visibility near cycle lanes. Unlike Wallaby, which tended to remain stuck behind the cycle lane and pavement edge, the Cryptic-Lime-Marlin model correctly edged forward beyond the cycle lane to improve visibility of approaching traffic. This created a much more natural and practical positioning behaviour for urban driving.

However, the model still felt overly cautious in several scenarios, particularly at junctions where hesitation led to missed opportunities and reduced traffic flow. While the behaviour remained safe, the lack of confidence could negatively affect customer perception and overall ride quality in real-world city driving. There were also occasional issues with incorrect early indication, where the model would activate indicators for turns it ultimately did not take before turning later at a different location.

Overall, the Cryptic-Lime-Marlin model showed meaningful improvements over Wallaby in comfort, width restriction handling, and junction visibility positioning. However, it still requires refinement in confidence, progression, and decision timing to provide a more natural and customer-ready urban driving experience.

Feedback on Cryptic-Lime-Marlin Model Performance – DWR Route

During testing on the DWR candidate demo route, the Cryptic-Lime-Marlin model delivered mixed overall performance. While there were noticeable improvements in some traffic response behaviours and lane merging decisions, several steering, positioning, and pedestrian interaction issues reduced overall confidence in the model’s consistency.

One of the most recurring concerns throughout the route was steering behaviour during left turns. Multiple instances of oversteering were observed, including a left turn from Tilliard Road where the vehicle steered excessively toward the curb before self-correcting. A similar issue occurred when turning from Western Rise onto Penton Road, where the model again oversteered left before recovering. Although the vehicle corrected itself in both scenarios, the steering inputs felt unnatural and reduced passenger confidence.

The model also struggled at the 7.2 width restriction. Instead of moving correctly into the shared lane, it failed to position itself adequately and made contact with the raised curb. Another positioning issue occurred when the model attempted an early lane change while an oncoming bus was still present. The correct behaviour would have been to continue forward first before initiating the turn movement.

Despite these issues, the model demonstrated some improvements in traffic response behaviour compared to previous runs. Reaction to amber traffic lights was smooth and controlled, avoiding the harsh braking seen in some other models. The vehicle also performed well after turning into Acton Street, merging back into the default lane promptly rather than delaying the merge unnecessarily. At a yellow box junction, the model correctly held back when space ahead was unclear, showing good caution and traffic awareness.

However, the model still lacked confidence and assertiveness in several scenarios. It failed to overtake a right-turning vehicle despite a safe opportunity being available, leading to unnecessary hesitation and eventually causing delay at an amber traffic light. Similar overly cautious behaviour was observed at give-way sections, particularly on Penton Rise, where the vehicle waited for complete traffic clearance rather than edging forward gradually to improve visibility and create opportunities to proceed. In busier situations, manual intervention was required to maintain traffic flow.

Positioning at the Wingford Road junction also requires improvement. The model remained too far back instead of edging forward for better visibility and safer exit opportunities onto the main road.

A more significant concern involved pedestrian interaction on Blundell Road at an informal crossing. The model continued assertively despite a pedestrian clearly committing to crossing based on body language and movement. In this scenario, the model should have yielded and come to a stop. This behaviour raises concern regarding pedestrian interpretation and safety, particularly in situations where no human oversight is available.

Overall, the Cryptic-Lime-Marlin model shows encouraging improvements in some traffic-flow behaviours and smoother traffic-light responses, but important refinement is still needed in steering precision, junction assertiveness, lane positioning, and pedestrian anticipation before the model can be considered reliable for demo or customer-facing use

Key Observations
Speed Limit Transition — Hamazakibashi Junction

At Hamazakibashi Junction, the speed limit changed from 50 km/h to 40 km/h, but the vehicle continued without reducing speed.

Impact:
This creates a speed compliance issue, especially in a junction area where road geometry usually requires earlier deceleration.

Desired Improvement:
The vehicle should recognize the speed limit change earlier and complete deceleration before entering the 40 km/h zone.

Late Highway Exit Maneuver — Shiodome Exit

At the Shiodome highway exit, the vehicle moved toward the exit only about 50 meters before the exit point.

Impact:
This felt highly unsafe due to the extremely short remaining distance. It could result in abrupt lane movement and create risk for surrounding vehicles.

Desired Improvement:
The vehicle should commit to the exit lane much earlier and avoid last-moment exit maneuvers.

Missed Required Lane Change After Route 15

After passing Route 15, the vehicle needed to move into the left lane, but it failed to do so.
Driver intervention was required.

Context:
If the vehicle continued straight, it would have entered the underpass.

Desired Improvement:
Route-based lane planning should identify this required left-lane positioning earlier and execute the lane change with sufficient margin.

Missed Right Turn Near Shimbashi Station Underpass

Near Shimbashi Station, under the railway overpass, the route required a right turn.
However, the vehicle did not activate the indicator and attempted to continue straight, requiring intervention.

Impact:
This is a route-following and signaling issue. Missing both the maneuver and the indicator reduces predictability and can cause route deviation.

Desired Improvement:
The vehicle should correctly identify the required right turn earlier and activate the indicator before the maneuver.

Tight Left Corner With Roadside Signboard

At a tight left corner, there was a roadside signboard ahead.
The vehicle was unable to complete the turn smoothly and required intervention.

Impact:
This suggests insufficient path planning around roadside obstacles in tight cornering scenarios.

Desired Improvement:
The vehicle should account for visible roadside obstacles and adjust entry speed / steering timing before entering tight turns.

Narrow Road Oncoming Vehicle Encounter

On a narrow road, the vehicle encountered an oncoming vehicle.

The vehicle should better judge lateral clearance and yield / proceed decisions when meeting oncoming vehicles on narrow roads.

Model Performance Review – Copper Panda Excited

Overview

Tested the “Copper Panda Excited” model on the SWR route. Performance was mixed, with the model demonstrating both assertive and cautious behaviours, although these were not always applied appropriately to the driving context. While several positive driving characteristics were observed, a number of safety-critical interventions were required.

Critical Safety Issues

* Overtook a cyclist twice on a narrow road while oncoming traffic was present:
    * First manoeuvre required intervention due to insufficient space with approaching vehicles.
    * Second manoeuvre was unnecessary given stationary traffic ahead.
* Failed to stop for a pedestrian with a dog waiting to cross at a zebra crossing on the elevated road, requiring intervention.
* Struck a dropped traffic cone on Offord Road instead of overtaking safely or yielding to oncoming traffic.
* Stopped with the rear wheel positioned on a zebra crossing; vehicle should have stopped between the yellow box and crossing.

Speed and Ride Comfort

* Excessive speed observed over speed bumps, travelling between 14–18 mph where approximately 12 mph would be more appropriate.
* Poor vehicle positioning over single speed bumps resulted in unnecessary cabin discomfort.
* Wheel placement should be improved to better distribute suspension load when traversing road features.
* New lane-positioning taxonomy for handling speed bumps and potholes may help improve ride quality beyond simply reducing speed.

Positive Behaviours

* Smooth and controlled stops at red lights and behind stationary vehicles with good distance management.
* Appropriate slowing behaviour for informal crossing locations.
* Successfully navigated a 6’6” width restriction.
* Demonstrated good assertive behaviour when exiting side roads where suitable gaps were available.
* Correctly applied cautious behaviour at a give-way scenario by edging forward, recognising that an approaching truck was not slowing, and holding position appropriately.

Technical Observations

* Strong regenerative braking characteristics noted throughout the route.
* Model did not react quickly enough to an oncoming vehicle flashing its lights to indicate priority.
* Lane positioning through a width restriction could be improved, with an unnecessary leftward angle observed.
* Failed to provide sufficient space for a delivery person with a trolley partially protruding into the roadway.
* Running on iGPU hardware yet still performed well across a number of scenarios.

Overall Assessment

The model demonstrates several promising behaviours, particularly in stopping smoothness, gap acceptance, and general vehicle control. However, the safety-critical issues observed during testing, particularly around vulnerable road users and overtaking decisions, would need to be addressed before consideration for demo or wider deployment use.

.

Model Performance Review – Copper-Panda-Excited (SWRV2)

Testing Context

* Second test attempt following an uncommanded reset and isolator reset.
* Route started from Brewery Road instead of Till Yard Road.
* Over-throttle behaviour appears to be active on this assignment.

Speed Management

Areas for Improvement

* The model traversed speed bumps at approximately 17 mph, creating noticeable cabin discomfort and placing unnecessary stress on the suspension.
* While the model accelerates efficiently back to the speed limit after speed bumps, it does not sufficiently reduce speed beforehand to provide a smooth transition.
* A more balanced approach to handling isolated speed bumps is required, combining appropriate deceleration with efficient recovery afterwards.

Positive Observations

* The model consistently returns to the posted speed limit quickly after obstacles and traffic-calming measures.

Positioning and Navigation

Areas for Improvement

* At the 6’6” width restriction, the vehicle positioned itself excessively to the left, leaving only 1–2 inches from the curb. This created a situation where driver intervention may have been required.
* At zebra crossings, the vehicle stopped with the rear wheels still occupying the crossing area rather than positioning correctly between the zebra crossing and the yellow box junction.
* During unprotected right turns, the rear of the vehicle frequently remained protruded into the adjacent lane, obstructing traffic approaching from behind.
    * The vehicle should achieve better alignment before entering the central reservation.
    * This behaviour was observed across multiple locations and does not appear to be site-specific.

Traffic Interaction

Areas for Improvement

* During an unprotected right turn, the model failed to hold position when a van blocked an oncoming vehicle. The system should have waited for the vehicles involved to negotiate the situation before proceeding.
* When merging from side roads, the model displayed excessive caution, waiting for three vehicles to pass despite an available safe and assertive gap.
* The model did not initiate an overtake of a stationary bus that was clearly switched off and unlikely to move.
    * Most models would typically edge forward to signal intent to oncoming traffic before executing the manoeuvre.

Positive Behaviour

* The model indicates earlier than most comparable models, providing clear communication of intent to other road users.
* Strong speed recovery behaviour after obstacles and traffic-calming features.
* Overall signalling behaviour contributes positively to road-user predictability and awareness.

Summary

Copper-Panda-Excited demonstrates strong intent communication through early signalling and effective speed recovery. However, improvements are required in speed management over traffic-calming features, positioning accuracy in constrained environments, execution of unprotected right turns, and overall confidence during traffic interactions. The model currently exhibits a combination of over-throttle tendencies and overly conservative decision-making in some negotiation scenarios, resulting in inconsistent driving behaviour that should be refined in future iterations.

However, there were several areas where the model's behaviour could be improved:

Traffic Signal, Route, and Lane Behavior Observations

* Despite the presence of a parked vehicle, the vehicle changed into the left lane and then cut in front of a vehicle that had originally been traveling ahead. While the maneuver itself was smooth, it suggests that parked vehicle recognition may still need improvement.
* Some longitudinal weight transfer was noticeable during speed maintenance.

Mercedes CLA Model Review

Model: Influential Manta Ray Magenta
Route: Free Plot

 Summary

The vehicle demonstrated several positive driving behaviours, including confident decision-making in dynamic traffic situations, successful navigation of constrained road features, and proactive signalling behaviour. However, the drive also exposed multiple intervention-level issues, including a safety-critical pedestrian detection failure and incorrect stopping behaviour at a signalised junction. These issues should be prioritised before further validation.

Critical Issues Requiring Intervention
1. Pedestrian Detection Failure (Safety Critical)

Severity: P0

The vehicle failed to recognise a pedestrian who was actively crossing at a zebra crossing.

No appropriate yielding behaviour was observed.
Intervention was required when the vehicle's front wheels reached the crossing.
The pedestrian was already established on the crossing prior to vehicle entry.

Assessment:
This was the most significant issue observed during the drive. The behaviour suggests a failure in perception, prediction, or crossing-priority handling and requires immediate investigation.

2. Traffic Light Stop Positioning

Severity: P1

The vehicle performed a controlled soft stop when approaching a red traffic signal but came to rest beyond the intended stopping position.

Vehicle encroached into the cyclist box.
Deceleration profile was smooth and comfortable.
Intervention was required to prevent further infringement.

Assessment:
While the stopping behaviour itself was well controlled, stopping distance and final stop position require recalibration to ensure compliance with road markings and cyclist infrastructure.

3. Route Execution Failure

Severity: P1

The vehicle initially failed to exit Tillyard Road as expected.

Manual intervention was required to continue the route.

Assessment:
This may indicate a planning, route-selection, or behavioural decision-making issue and should be reviewed against route logs.

4. Throttle Pedal Control Non-Responsiveness

Severity: P1

Throttle pedal controls were non-responsive throughout the test.

Assessment:
This prevented assessment of pedal interaction functionality and should be investigated before future testing.

Driving Quality Observations
Traffic Light Behaviour

Strengths

Smooth and controlled deceleration approaching the signal.
Soft-stop behaviour contributed to passenger comfort.

Areas for Improvement

Increase stopping margin before stop lines and cyclist boxes.
Improve final stop position accuracy.
Speed Bump Handling

Observation

The vehicle's chosen path resulted in one side of the vehicle traversing the speed bump while the other side largely avoided it.

Impact

Generated unnecessary cabin disturbance.
Delivered a less comfortable ride than would typically be expected from an experienced human driver.

Recommendation

Review path planning and comfort optimisation around traffic-calming features.
Positive Performance Highlights
Navigation and Vehicle Placement

The vehicle successfully negotiated several challenging road features without intervention:

Completed the chicane cleanly.
Successfully navigated the 6'6" width restriction.
Maintained appropriate vehicle positioning throughout.
Signalling Behaviour
Indicators were activated early and consistently.
Signalling behaviour improved predictability for surrounding road users.
Early indication is preferred and aligns with good driving practice.
Roundabout Decision Making

The vehicle demonstrated appropriate assertiveness at a roundabout.

Correctly interpreted hesitation from a vehicle with nominal priority.
Progressed safely rather than waiting unnecessarily.
Behaviour closely matched the expectations of a confident human driver.

Assessment:
This was one of the strongest aspects of the drive and reflects positive progress in interaction with other road users.


The model demonstrated encouraging performance in navigation, signalling, and interaction with other road users, particularly in situations requiring confidence and initiative. However, the test also revealed several intervention-level failures, including a safety-critical pedestrian detection issue. Resolving these safety and compliance concerns should be the immediate priority, while preserving the positive behavioural characteristics observed throughout the drive.

Mercedes CLA Autonomous Vehicle Test Review

Model: Influential Manta Ray Magenta
Configuration: Bold Mode vs Balanced Mode
Route: Free Plot

Summary

This test focused on evaluating the vehicle in Bold Mode compared with Balanced Mode. The model showed improved confidence and decisiveness, particularly in situations where previous Balanced Mode runs hesitated. However, several safety-critical issues and reliability concerns were identified that require attention before wider use.

⸻

Critical Safety Issues

Zebra Crossing Failure (P0)

The vehicle failed to stop for a pedestrian who was already crossing at a zebra crossing.

* The vehicle appeared to hesitate but continued through the crossing.
* Intervention was required.

Assessment: Pedestrian priority should be maintained regardless of driving mode and requires immediate investigation.

Jaywalker Response (P0)

The vehicle failed to slow down or take a precautionary approach when encountering a jaywalker.

Assessment: The model needs to be more cautious around pedestrians whose intentions may be uncertain.

Delivery Van Passing (P0)

The vehicle passed a delivery van with an open side door at an inappropriate speed.

* The model did not sufficiently account for the possibility of a person stepping into the road.

Assessment: Slower speeds should be adopted around vehicles associated with pedestrian activity.

⸻

Bold Mode vs Balanced Mode

Improvements

* More decisive than Balanced Mode.
* Successfully exited Tillyard Road without the hesitation seen in previous runs.
* Appropriately crept forward to improve visibility before proceeding.

Areas for Improvement

* Shorter following distances behind vehicles.
* Later reactions to developing situations.
* Faster speed over speed bumps, reducing passenger comfort.
* Executed a very wide turn when exiting Tillyard Road, briefly entering the oncoming lane.

Assessment: Bold Mode improves confidence and progress but currently reduces comfort and safety margins more than desired.

⸻

Reliability Issues

Uncommanded Event

An uncommanded event occurred during the drive and requires investigation.

Move-Off Failure

After re-engagement from a standstill, the vehicle failed to move off correctly.

* A throttle tap was required to recover.

Traffic Light Move-Off

The vehicle failed to proceed on a green light for an unprotected right turn.

* Intervention required.

Roundabout Behaviour

Intervention was required due to uncertainty around right-of-way handling.

Overtake Failure

The vehicle failed to initiate an overtake around a stationary vehicle at the Brandon Road exit.

⸻

Positive Observations

* More confident and decisive than Balanced Mode.
* Reduced hesitation in challenging scenarios.
* Better use of creeping behaviour to gain visibility before proceeding.
* Demonstrated improved willingness to make progress when safe opportunities were available.

⸻

Recommended Actions

High Priority

* Investigate zebra crossing failure.
* Improve handling of jaywalkers and vulnerable road users.
* Reduce passing speeds around parked and delivery vehicles.
* Review Bold Mode safety limits to ensure assertiveness does not compromise pedestrian safety.

Medium Priority

* Investigate move-off failures and uncommanded events.
* Improve traffic-light move-off behaviour.
* Review roundabout decision making and overtake logic.

Low Priority

* Increase following distances.
* Improve comfort over speed bumps.
* Refine turning path to avoid encroaching into opposing lanes.

⸻

Overall Assessment

Bold Mode is a clear improvement in terms of confidence and decisiveness, successfully addressing some of the hesitation observed in Balanced Mode. However, the current level of assertiveness can reduce safety margins and passenger comfort, particularly around pedestrians and vulnerable road users. Resolving these safety-critical issues should be the priority before further testing or demonstrations.

column4: Testing CLA baseline model on Free plot
column58:
Mercedes CLA Autonomous Vehicle Test Review

Model: Apricot Vociferous Elk
Configuration: Balanced Mode
Objective: Comparison against current baseline model

Summary

The Apricot Vociferous Elk model demonstrated a noticeable improvement over the current baseline during testing. The vehicle showed stronger decision-making, improved handling around challenging scenarios, and more natural driving behaviour overall. The model felt more comfortable and demo-ready, requiring fewer interventions and displaying greater confidence when navigating the route.

While some driving quality and path-planning improvements remain, the overall performance represents a significant step forward compared with the current baseline.

⸻

Key Improvements Over Baseline

Tillyard Road Exit

One of the most notable improvements was the successful navigation of the Tillyard Road exit.

* Completed the manoeuvre without intervention.
* Demonstrated appropriate creeping behaviour to improve visibility before proceeding.
* Baseline model typically hesitates or requires intervention in this scenario.

Improved Crossing and Stopping Behaviour

* Better handling of zebra crossings and stopping scenarios.
* More consistent and predictable stopping behaviour than the baseline model.

Increased Environmental Awareness

* Demonstrated greater caution around tight spaces and constrained environments.
* Better vehicle positioning when approaching challenging road features.

More Natural Progression

* Used creeping behaviour appropriately when visibility was limited.
* Avoided the tendency of the baseline model to remain static in uncertain situations.

⸻

Driving Quality Observations

Comfort and Ride Quality

The vehicle delivered a comfortable driving experience throughout the route.

* Smoother overall ride quality.
* Handled speed bumps effectively.
* Felt more refined than the current Mackie vehicles.

Width Restrictions

* Successfully navigated width restrictions without issue.
* Maintained good control and confidence through narrow sections.

Roundabout Performance

* Demonstrated good speed control.
* Maintained approximately 9 mph through roundabouts where appropriate.
* Progressed confidently without unnecessary hesitation.

⸻

Areas for Improvement

Lane Positioning

* Vehicle occasionally favoured one side of the lane.
* More balanced lane centring would improve overall driving quality.

Turning Behaviour

* Some wider-than-desired turns observed when entering PED areas.
* Path planning could be refined to improve positioning through these manoeuvres.

Give-Way Behaviour

* Vehicle occasionally appeared hesitant when moving off from give-way positions.
* At times seemed stuck between waiting and committing to acceleration.

⸻

Demo Readiness Assessment

The Apricot model appears significantly more suitable for demonstration drives than the current baseline.

Strengths

* Requires fewer interventions.
* More confident and predictable behaviour.
* Better comfort and passenger experience.
* Eliminates several issues regularly observed with the baseline model.

Considerations

* Route adjustments may help showcase the model’s strengths more effectively.
* Central London and British Museum routes have not yet been evaluated and should be assessed before broader deployment.

⸻

Software Considerations

During discussion, concerns were raised that some recent baseline model issues may be influenced by software changes rather than model performance alone.

* Current performance issues appear to have emerged around the end of March.
* Previous February builds were considered more stable.
* Further investigation may be required to understand the contribution of software changes to recent regressions.

⸻

Recommended Actions

High Priority

* Continue validation of the Apricot model for demo readiness.
* Evaluate performance on Central London and British Museum routes.

Medium Priority

* Improve lane centring and positioning.
* Refine turning behaviour in PED entry scenarios.
* Improve confidence when moving off from give-way positions.

Longer Term

* Review software regressions introduced since February.
* Consider route modifications that better showcase the model’s strengths during demonstrations.

⸻

Overall Assessment

The Apricot Vociferous Elk model represents a clear improvement over the current baseline. It demonstrated stronger decision-making, improved handling of complex scenarios, and a more comfortable overall driving experience. While some refinement is still required around lane positioning and move-off behaviour, the model appears substantially closer to demo readiness and is currently one of the strongest-performing configurations evaluated on this route

The model handled most aspects very well this time, including width restrictions and jaywalking scenarios. It felt confident and assertive while still maintaining an appropriate level of caution, which worked well. Overall, this was the best run I've seen in the past few days.

Model Performance Review – Cryptic Lime Marlin

Test Overview

Cryptic Lime Marlin was tested during a reconnaissance run for an upcoming demo. Overall, the model performed adequately and is suitable for the demonstration. It handled most scenarios safely but remains more cautious than would be expected for typical London driving.

Positive Performance

* Demonstrated good traffic judgement when exiting Tillyard Road, creeping forward for visibility before merging.
* Handled temporary traffic lights appropriately.
* Showed strong hazard detection and pedestrian awareness.
* Slowed appropriately for a closed zebra crossing with limited visibility.
* Smoothly reduced speed for a pedestrian approaching from the right.
* Successfully navigated multiple narrow roads, chicanes, and width restrictions.
* Performed well in busy environments with cyclists, pedestrians, and parked vehicles.
* Made sensible decisions in traffic, including giving way to avoid blocking a side road.
* Successfully managed cyclists approaching from both sides during an unprotected right turn.

Areas for Improvement

Positioning and Turning

* Slight oversteer observed during a late left turn on Tillyard Road.
* Very late positioning adjustment approaching the third width restriction, resulting in a near curb strike.
* Passed a cyclist with less clearance than desirable in a constrained section of road.

Junction Behaviour

* Failed to respond appropriately to flashing headlights from an oncoming vehicle.
* Displayed excessive hesitation at give-way sections despite safe opportunities to proceed.
* Slow to react to vehicles indicating turns, resulting in missed opportunities to make progress.

Speed and Acceleration

* Requires more throttle when exiting junctions.
* Relies too heavily on braking rather than using acceleration when appropriate.
* Driving style remains overly cautious for London traffic conditions.

Summary

Cryptic Lime Marlin demonstrated strong hazard perception, pedestrian awareness, and the ability to safely navigate complex urban environments. The model handled challenging road layouts and busy traffic situations well. The main area for improvement is confidence and assertiveness, particularly at junctions and during interactions with other road users. While safe and predictable, the model can be hesitant in situations where more decisive action would improve traffic flow

column4: Recce Regular testing of the model
column58:
Model Performance Review

Test Overview

This session focused on comparing vehicle  25 and vehicle 28 across a range of urban driving scenarios.

Vehicle 25 generally demonstrated calmer and more measured behaviour but occasionally lacked the immediate response required in dynamic situations. Model 28 was noticeably more assertive, although at times this translated into overly aggressive behaviour, particularly around vulnerable road users.

Positive Performance

* vehicle 25 displayed a smoother and more controlled driving style overall.
* Both models were capable of identifying overtaking opportunities, although execution was inconsistent.
* vehicle 28 showed improved willingness to make progress compared with vehicle 25 in certain traffic situations.

Safety-Critical Issues

Cyclist Interaction

* vehicle 28 demonstrated overly aggressive behaviour around cyclists.
* On Camden Street, the vehicle followed a cyclist extremely closely between roadworks and the pavement, creating an uncomfortable and potentially unsafe situation.
* Similar behaviour was observed in the Gospel Oak area, where the model appeared to pressure cyclists rather than maintain appropriate spacing.

Pedestrian Awareness

* At a zebra crossing, the model proceeded through without reducing speed despite a pedestrian being partially obscured between parked vehicles and oncoming traffic occupying the crossing area.
* In a separate jaywalker scenario near the triangle island, the model failed to react appropriately to pedestrians crossing two lanes of traffic.
* While a collision was not imminent, the vehicle should have reduced speed in response to the developing situation.

Positioning and Manoeuvring

Turning Performance

* vehicle 28 oversteered during a left turn from Tillyard Road and did not self-correct quickly enough.

Width Restrictions

* The model failed a width restriction manoeuvre by continuing towards the raised pavement instead of moving into the correct lane.
* The issue occurred despite indicator use.
* Successful navigation was only achieved when the vehicle was manually aligned beforehand.

Lane Positioning

* The model struggled with lane positioning in narrow road sections and when passing static obstacles.
* Positioning confidence through constrained areas requires further improvement.

Traffic Interaction and Speed Management

* Several “fail to overtake” scenarios were observed where the model did not initiate an overtake despite safe opportunities being available.
* At give-way sections, the model reduced speed too early and remained too far back from the junction.
* Positioning closer to the main road would improve visibility and create better merging opportunities.
* In one instance, the model failed to accelerate and make progress after a van had passed, despite a clear opportunity being available.

Summary

Vehicle 25 delivered a calmer and more predictable driving experience, although it occasionally lacked responsiveness. Model 28 showed improved willingness to make progress but introduced several concerns relating to cyclist interactions, pedestrian awareness, and positioning accuracy.

The most significant areas requiring improvement are cyclist proximity management, pedestrian detection and response, width restriction navigation, and confidence when negotiating narrow urban environments. While Model 28 demonstrated stronger assertiveness, further refinement is required to ensure that progress is achieved without compromising safety or comfort.

column4: Testing baseline on British museum route
column58:
Model Performance Review – Cryptic-Lime-Marlin

Route: British Museum via Sidmouth

Test Overview

Cryptic-Lime-Marlin delivered a strong overall performance throughout the route and handled many of the complex urban driving scenarios confidently and smoothly. The model demonstrated good awareness of vulnerable road users and generally maintained appropriate speeds through traffic-calming features and constrained road environments.

Positive Performance

Pedestrian Awareness

* One of the standout behaviours was the model’s ability to identify pedestrians who appeared likely to cross the road.
* Rather than reacting abruptly, the vehicle reduced speed smoothly and progressively, creating a comfortable and natural driving experience.
* Pedestrian interactions felt well anticipated and controlled throughout the route.

Traffic Negotiation

* The model performed well when negotiating through traffic and demonstrated good judgement in complex situations.
* Decision-making was generally appropriate and allowed steady progress.
* Some throttle assistance was required on occasion to help the vehicle commit to manoeuvres and maintain momentum.

Speed Management

* Speed selection was appropriate across most of the route.
* The model handled speed bumps comfortably and maintained good control through width restrictions.
* General road speed management was consistent and well balanced.

Areas for Improvement

Steering Correction on Left Turns

* On several sharper left turns, the model struggled to make timely steering corrections.
* This resulted in the vehicle tracking too close to the kerb before recovering.
* An intervention was required at the beginning of the run when exiting Tillyard Road to prevent a potential kerb strike.
* Similar behaviour was observed on other tighter left-hand turns, suggesting an area for further refinement.

Summary

Cryptic-Lime-Marlin performed well overall and handled the majority of complex scenarios on this route with confidence. Strong pedestrian awareness, smooth speed management, and effective traffic negotiation were key strengths. The primary area requiring improvement is steering correction during sharper left turns, where the model occasionally failed to recover its line quickly enough. Aside from this, the model delivered a safe, comfortable, and well-controlled

column4: This was an assignment for licensing
column58:
Model Performance Review: Apricot-Fish-Cordial (UK Potential Licensing Route)

Model Performance Overview:

* The model handled the basics well. It consistently aimed for appropriate speed limits (20, 30, 40, 50 mph) and on motorways, reached around 68 mph. Lane positioning and general road compliance were solid. Stops at traffic lights or behind stationary traffic were smooth, contributing to passenger comfort.

Positive Aspects:

* The model’s commitment to reaching speed limits was appreciated, giving consistent performance across different zones.
* The model’s stopping was smooth and controlled, adding comfort during traffic light stops and behind stationary vehicles.

Areas for Improvement:

* In slow-moving traffic, instead of creeping forward gently, the model sometimes used excessive throttle, followed by braking shortly after. This led to a “stop-and-go” feel that could be smoother.
* In multi-lane situations, the model lacked eagerness to overtake slow traffic in adjacent lanes. It did not show intent to change lanes or proactively manage overtaking, which could improve its adaptability to London traffic flow.

Conclusion:
The model delivered a solid baseline—speed adherence, lane positioning, and smooth stops were strengths. To improve, it needs smoother low-speed movement and more assertiveness in overtaking scenarios. These refinements will help it handle real-world traffic flow more naturally.

column4: Testing new model on candidate routes
column58:
Feedback on Apricot-Fish-Cordial Model Performance – SWR V2 Route

Model Performance Overview

Testing the Apricot-Fish-Cordial model on the SWR V2 route revealed a cautious but generally capable autonomous driving system. Throughout the route, the model demonstrated good situational awareness, reliable navigation, and smooth handling in many scenarios. However, it consistently required throttle assistance in situations where a more confident decision to proceed would have been appropriate.

The overall driving style felt predictable and controlled, which is preferable to models that exhibit unexpected or inconsistent behaviour. However, the model’s tendency toward excessive caution limited progression and occasionally affected traffic flow.

Positive Performance Observations

One of the model’s strongest attributes was its awareness of surrounding road users. On Brewery Road, the vehicle correctly held back for oncoming traffic while simultaneously tracking a cyclist approaching from the left. Rather than proceeding prematurely, it waited until the cyclist had fully passed before continuing, demonstrating good situational awareness and hazard assessment.

The model also reacted appropriately when encountering a reversing Peugeot whose front end protruded into the road. It adjusted its position slightly to the right to maintain a safe clearance without overreacting, showing good spatial awareness.

Navigation performance was another strong area. The model consistently selected the correct lanes for upcoming turns and route requirements without hesitation, avoiding many of the lane-selection issues seen in other models. Speed management was generally smooth, particularly around zebra crossings where the vehicle slowed early and progressively, resulting in comfortable and natural stops. Speed bump handling was also balanced, with the vehicle maintaining reasonable cabin comfort while traversing them.

Areas Requiring Improvement

The most significant issue observed throughout the route was hesitation at decision points, particularly when joining main roads from side roads or navigating give-way situations. Even when large and clearly safe gaps were available, the model frequently chose to wait rather than proceed. This behaviour required throttle assistance on multiple occasions, including when exiting Tillyard Road and at several give-way junctions throughout the route.

A more concerning incident occurred at a yellow box junction. The model stopped briefly with two wheels positioned within the yellow box area, creating a situation that could potentially result in a traffic offence. Immediate throttle intervention was required. In this scenario, the model should have either committed to the manoeuvre earlier or crept forward to improve visibility before making a decision.

Overall Assessment

The Apricot-Fish-Cordial model demonstrates strong situational awareness, reliable navigation, and smooth vehicle control. Its cautious nature makes it predictable and relatively easy to work with during testing and demos. However, the level of hesitation observed in junction and give-way scenarios currently limits its effectiveness in real-world traffic conditions.

While the model rarely puts itself in difficult situations, it would benefit from increased confidence and improved progression when safe opportunities are available. Further testing in a more assertive driving mode, such as Bold mode, may help address these shortcomings while retaining the positive awareness and smoothness characteristics already present.

Overall, the model shows good potential but requires refinement in decision-making and junction confidence before it can deliver a more natural and efficient driving experience.

Feedback on Apricot-Fish-Cordial Model Performance – British Museum Route via Sidmouth (09:15 Demo)

During the 09:15 demo on the British Museum route via Sidmouth, the Apricot-Fish-Cordial model delivered a smooth and comfortable drive overall. Performance on vehicle FME20025 felt noticeably refined, with smooth acceleration, braking, and general vehicle control throughout the route. The model maintained good traffic awareness, appropriate following distances, and demonstrated the improved confidence seen in Bold Mode testing.

One recurring characteristic observed was a tendency to initiate some turns slightly late, resulting in mild oversteering toward the curb before the vehicle corrected itself. While the model consistently recovered in time and completed the manoeuvres safely, the steering behaviour still felt slightly unnatural and would benefit from further refinement.

Overall, the model performed strongly and left a positive impression from both a comfort and driving behaviour perspective.

Feedback on Apricot-Fish-Cordial Model Performance – SWR V2 Route (11:05 Demo)

During the 11:05 demo on the SWR V2 route, the Apricot-Fish-Cordial model delivered another strong performance. Speed management, lane positioning, pedestrian interactions, and general traffic integration were all handled confidently and smoothly.

As observed in previous runs, there were occasional examples of late turn initiation which resulted in slight oversteering. However, the model consistently corrected itself quickly and safely before any intervention was required. While not an immediate safety concern, the steering behaviour remains an area that could be further refined to improve overall smoothness and passenger confidence.

Outside of this characteristic, the route was completed without any notable concerns and the model remained predictable and comfortable throughout.

Feedback on Apricot-Fish-Cordial Model Performance – SWR V2 Route (11:38 Demo)

During the 11:38 demo on the SWR V2 route, the Apricot-Fish-Cordial model again demonstrated strong overall capability, maintaining smooth driving behaviour, good lane positioning, and appropriate speed management throughout most of the route.

A notable issue occurred at a width restriction where the model attempted to overtake a Luton van. The manoeuvre was not appropriate for the available space and required intervention to prevent the vehicle from continuing with the overtake. Aside from this isolated incident, the route was completed confidently and smoothly.

The model also continued to display the occasional late-turn characteristic seen in previous runs. This resulted in brief oversteering toward the inside of tighter turns before correcting itself. The corrections were timely and effective, but the behaviour remains noticeable and could be improved.

Overall, the model performed well, with the attempted overtake being the primary intervention-worthy event during the run.

**Key Observations**

* **GPS signal loss / route-following failure**
  The vehicle lost GPS signal and was no longer able to follow the planned route.
  A power cycle was required to recover the system.

* **Late lane change decision at highway split**
  At a highway split, the vehicle was traveling in the far-left lane but did not initiate the required lane change even when the split was less than 200 meters away.

* **DILC intervention performed**
  DILC was performed to avoid a late or unsafe lane change near the highway split.

* **Guest perception**
  The guest did not appear to notice the intervention, so the maneuver did not negatively impact the demo experience from the passenger perspective.

---

## **Summary of Main Demo Risks**

### **High Priority**

1. GPS signal loss causing route-following failure
2. Required lane change not initiated before a highway split
3. Risk of late lane change near a highway branch

### **Positive Point**

* DILC intervention was smooth enough that the guest did not notice.

## **Key Observations**

### **Unsafe Lane Change Attempt Over Yellow Line on Highway**

While driving on the highway, the vehicle moved from the center lane to the left lane.
This initial lane change was appropriate and felt like a good decision.

However, shortly after that, the vehicle attempted to move back toward the center lane while crossing a **yellow line**, which required intervention.

**Impact:**
This was an unsafe and unexpected maneuver. The intent of the second lane change was unclear, especially because the first lane change had been appropriate.

**Priority:** High

---

### **Failure to Return to Correct Lane After Avoiding Roadworks**

To avoid a roadwork area, the vehicle moved into the oncoming lane.
However, after avoiding the construction zone, it continued driving in the oncoming lane as if following the vehicle ahead, rather than returning to the correct driving lane.
Intervention was required.

**Additional Note:**
This location has shown the same issue in other runs as well. The vehicle often continues driving in the oncoming lane and does not return to the proper lane after passing the roadwork area.

**Impact:**
This is a recurring and high-risk behavior. Continuing in the oncoming lane after the avoidance maneuver creates a serious safety concern.

**Priority:** Critical

---

### **Unsafe Double Lane Change With Incorrect Indicator**

Near the end of the route, the vehicle made a right turn and then needed to prepare for a left turn ahead.
At this point, the vehicle activated the **right indicator** while moving toward the **left lane**, resulting in a double lane change.

**Impact:**
This behavior was unsafe and confusing for surrounding traffic because the indicated direction did not match the actual vehicle movement.
The double lane change also increased risk, especially near a route transition point.

**Priority:** Critical

---

# **Summary of Main Demo Risks**

### **Critical**

1. Continued driving in the oncoming lane after avoiding roadworks
2. Unsafe double lane change with incorrect indicator direction

### **High**

3. Attempted lane change over a yellow line on the highway
4. Unclear lane-change intent after an initially appropriate maneuver

---

## **Key Observations**

### **No Recurrence of Earlier Issues**

During this ride, the issues observed in the previous rides from the same day were not clearly reproduced.

**Positive Observation:**
This was a good improvement compared with the earlier rides, and the overall behavior felt more stable.

---

### **Late Lane Change Timing Near Highway Splits**

However, lane changes for highway splits were still performed only when the remaining distance became quite short.

In a normal VSO run, this would likely require intervention.
If a guest were onboard during a demo, this type of late lane change could feel unsafe or uncomfortable.

**Impact:**
Late lane changes near highway splits reduce confidence and may create anxiety for passengers, even if the maneuver is completed successfully.

**Desired Improvement:**
The vehicle should prepare for highway splits earlier and complete lane positioning with more distance margin.

**Priority:** High

---

### **Highway Exit Lane Change After Yellow Line**

Before exiting the highway, the vehicle waited until the yellow line ended before performing the lane change.
This was a good and compliant behavior.

However, the vehicle stayed in the center lane for too long before that point.
Ideally, the vehicle should have moved into the correct lane earlier, before reaching the section where the yellow line begins.

**Impact:**
Although the final lane change was legal and well-timed relative to the yellow line, earlier lane positioning would make the exit maneuver feel smoother and safer.

**Desired Improvement:**
The vehicle should anticipate the exit earlier and complete lane positioning before entering yellow-line constrained sections.

**Priority:** Moderate–High

---

# **Summary of Main Demo Feedback**

### **Positive**

1. Previous issues from earlier rides on the same day were not clearly reproduced.
2. The vehicle waited until the yellow line ended before changing lanes near the highway exit.

### **Needs Improvement**

1. Lane changes near highway splits still occur too late.
2. The vehicle should prepare for highway exits earlier, before reaching yellow-line sections.
3. Late lane changes may feel unsafe or uncomfortable to demo guests.

## **Key Observations**

### **Incorrect Indicator at First Intersection**

At the first intersection after start, the planned route was to go straight.
However, the vehicle activated the **left indicator**.

After DILC was applied, the indicator turned off and the vehicle continued straight as intended.

**Impact:**
The incorrect indicator could confuse surrounding traffic and reduce confidence in the vehicle’s route understanding.

**Priority:** Moderate–High

---

### **Failure to Follow Underpass Route**

The planned route required the vehicle to enter the underpass while indicating right.
However, the vehicle did not proceed toward the underpass.

Even after DILC was applied, the vehicle still did not move forward as expected.

**Impact:**
This suggests a route-following or path-commitment issue. It may require operator intervention if the vehicle cannot recover through DILC.

**Priority:** High

---

### **Unclear Stop Compliance**

There was one location where the stop behavior at a stop-controlled junction felt uncertain.

**Impact:**
Even if the vehicle may have partially stopped, stop compliance should be clear and unambiguous, especially in a demo context.

**Priority:** Moderate–High

---

# **Summary of Main Demo Risks**

### **High**

1. Failure to follow the planned underpass route
2. DILC did not recover the expected route-following behavior

3. Incorrect left indicator while the planned route was straight
4. Unclear stop compliance at one stop-controlled location

Route Display / UI Freeze

After starting the ride, the route color did not update after the vehicle passed through the route.
The route remained displayed as a blue line, even though the vehicle was already progressing along it.

The next-turn guidance window also appeared to remain frozen.

Impact:
This may confuse the operator and reduce confidence in whether the system is correctly tracking route progress.

Priority: High

Slight Jittery Motion Around 40 kph

Overall driving performance during this ride was extremely smooth.
However, while driving at around 40 kph, the vehicle showed a slight jittery motion.

This behavior was not observed at other speed ranges, so it may be a speed-specific issue.

Impact:
The issue was minor, but because it appears to occur only around 40 kph, it may be worth checking for speed-band-specific control behavior.

Priority: Moderate

GPS Acquisition Failure After Leaving Garage

After leaving the garage, the vehicle was unable to acquire GPS.
Although the blue light turned on, the vehicle did not follow the route and did not become healthy.

A power cycle was required to recover the system.

Impact:
This type of startup / GPS acquisition issue is particularly important for demo readiness.
If this happens frequently during live demo preparation, it can delay the demo and reduce operational confidence.

Desired Improvement:
The system should acquire GPS and reach a healthy state more reliably after leaving the garage, especially before demo operations.

Priority: High

Good Behavior During Thunderstorm / Traffic Light Outage

Due to a sudden thunderstorm, the traffic lights shut down.
Despite this unusual situation, the vehicle showed strong behavior.

It reacted appropriately to the movement of the vehicle ahead, and when it was the lead vehicle, it attempted to stop properly.

Positive Observation:
This was a very good response to an abnormal traffic-light condition and helped maintain confidence in the system.

Priority: Positive / Notable

Smooth and Confident Driving Overall

During the rest of the ride, the vehicle drove smoothly without creating discomfort or anxiety.

Positive Observation:
Overall ride quality was stable and confidence-inspiring.

The demo went very well. We had 4 cars on site (1 demo driver and 3 VSOs). There were 2 guests and 1 host in each vehicle.

The model handled every situation well. The only thing that was slightly noticeable was that the steering wheel moved back and forth a few times when braking below 2 km/h.

We also had to use DILC a few times, which the guests actually liked because they appreciated that this function exists.

The model reacted well to pedestrians and also showed very good performance in tight spaces.

The demo went well. I had to intervene once because we failed to yield to a vehicle approaching from the left.

There were a total of four drives. Since the guests had less time available than originally planned, we had to cut the route short and return to the site each time.

As we were using Google Maps, this was not an issue because we were able to adjust the route during the drive. The guests appreciated this flexibility, as it allowed us to adapt to their time constraints.

As a result, we ended up driving routes that we had not been able to test beforehand. One of the routes also consisted entirely of motorway driving, which we had not been able to test in advance either, as it was specifically requested by the customer.

Overall, it was a very exciting demo with many unexpected situations and surprises, all of which we handled very well.
